questions.text: At which stage of HIV infection does one usually show symptoms of AIDS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Within 15 days of sexual contact with an infected person
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because AIDS symptoms do not appear so soon after initial infection. HIV progresses through stages before reaching AIDS.

options.text: When the infected retrovirus enters host cells
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Although the virus enters host cells early in the infection, it takes time for enough damage to occur to lead to AIDS symptoms.

options.text: When HIV damages a large number of helper T-lymphocytes
options.explanation: This option is correct. AIDS symptoms typically appear when HIV has significantly reduced the number of CD4 cells, compromising the immune system.

options.text: When the viral DNA is produced by reverse transcriptase
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The production of viral DNA is an early step in HIV infection, occurring long before the onset of AIDS symptoms.

questions.explanation: AIDS is a condition that arises when the immune system is severely compromised due to a significant reduction in CD4 cells, which are targeted by the HIV virus. HIV initially infects the host by entering cells and using its reverse transcriptase to integrate its genetic material into the host's DNA. Over time, as the number of helper T-lymphocytes (CD4 cells) is depleted, the immune system weakens, leading to the symptoms associated with AIDS. This progressive damage does not occur immediately after infection or at the initial entry of the virus into cells, but rather after a significant loss of CD4 cells.

questions.text: Cirrhosis of the liver is caused by the chronic intake of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Opium has powerful effects on the brain and body because it contains alkaloids like morphine, codeine, and thebaine. While it may give temporary pain relief or euphoria, regular or prolonged use causes serious harm.
This option is correct because cirrhosis of the liver is most commonly caused by chronic and excessive alcohol consumption.
Chewing tobacco (also called smokeless tobacco) may seem less harmful than smoking, but it causes serious health problems because it contains nicotine and many cancer-causing chemicals.
Cocaine is a powerful stimulant drug that directly affects the brain and body. While it produces short-term euphoria and energy, it has severe harmful effects on almost every organ system.

questions.explanation: Cirrhosis is a condition in which the liver responds to injury or death of some of its cells by producing interlacing strands of fibrous tissue between which are nodules of regenerating cells. The liver becomes tawny and characteristically knobbly (due to the nodules). One of the causes includes alcoholism (alcoholic cirrhosis). Chronic infection with hepatitis B or hepatitis C viruses can lead to liver inflammation and fibrosis, eventually progressing to cirrhosis.

questions.text: Infectious proteins are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because geminiviruses are a group of plant-infecting viruses characterized by their unique twin (geminate) particle structure. They do not consist of proteins alone, as viruses typically have a protein coat and genetic material.

This option is correct because prions are infectious agents made up of misfolded proteins. They can lead to neurodegenerative diseases by promoting the misfolding of normal proteins in the brain, making them unique among infectious agents.

This option is incorrect because viroids are the smallest known infectious agents and consist solely of short strands of circular, single-stranded RNA. They lack a protein coat and do not involve proteins in their structure.

This option is incorrect because satellite viruses are subviral agents that depend on a helper virus for replication. They do not consist solely of proteins, as they require a viral coat and cannot independently function.

questions.explanation: This option is correct because Prions are the infectious agents composed of abnormal misfolded proteins that have the ability to induce similar misfolding of normal proteins in the brain.

questions.text: Which of the following is a pair of viral diseases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the common cold is caused by the common cold virus (rhinovirus), and AIDS is caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus).
This option is incorrect because the common cold is caused by viruses, whereas dysentery can be caused by either bacteria or parasites, but not viruses.
This option is incorrect because typhoid is caused by Salmonella typhi. Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Typhoid and tuberculosis are bacterial diseases, not viral.
This option is incorrect because AIDS is a viral disease caused by the HIV virus. On the other hand, ringworm is a fungal disease, not viral.

questions.explanation: The common cold is a viral disease. It is caused by rhinoviruses. It causes fever and pain all over the body and affects the nose, throat, and air passages. AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome) is a disorder of the cell-mediated immune system of the body. It is caused by HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). HIV is a retrovirus that attacks helper T cells.

questions.text: The causative agent of mad cow disease is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mad cow disease is not caused by a virus. Viruses contain genetic material (DNA or RNA) and replicate within host cells. In contrast, the causative agent of mad cow disease lacks any genetic material and spreads through the misfolding of proteins, which is characteristic of prions.

Mad cow disease is not the result of a bacterial infection. Bacteria are living organisms that contain DNA and typical cellular structures, which are absent in prions. The agent responsible for mad cow disease is a prion, a misfolded protein that induces abnormal folding in normal proteins, unlike bacteria that can be treated with antibiotics.

The correct answer is prion, as mad cow disease, or bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE), is caused by these infectious proteins. Prions are unique because they do not contain nucleic acids; instead, they propagate by inducing normal proteins in the brain to misfold, leading to severe neurological damage.

Worms, such as helminths, are parasitic organisms that do not cause mad cow disease. They are multicellular organisms that can infect hosts, but they are fundamentally different from prions, which are solely misfolded proteins, lacking any cellular structure.

questions.explanation: Prions are infectious proteins that cause abnormal folding of normal brain proteins, leading to brain damage.

questions.text: HIV, which causes AIDS, first starts destroying:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome), a syndrome, is caused by the retrovirus HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). The virus destroys a subgroup of lymphocytes, the helper T cells (or CD4 lymphocytes), resulting in suppression of the body’s immune response.
B lymphocytes are also affected by HIV, but HIV primarily targets T lymphocytes.
Leukocytes are also affected by HIV, but HIV targets T-lymphocytes primarily.
This option is wrong, as HIV, which causes AIDS, first starts destroying helper T-lymphocytes.

questions.explanation: AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome), a syndrome, is caused by the retrovirus HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). The virus destroys a subgroup of lymphocytes, the helper T cells (or CD4 lymphocytes), resulting in suppression of the body’s immune response. HIV is transmitted in blood, semen, and vaginal fluid; the major routes of infection are unprotected vaginal and anal intercourse, intravenous drug abuse, and the administration of contaminated blood and blood products. A combination of antiviral drugs can delay the development of full-blown AIDS for many years but cannot fully cure the disease.

questions.text: If a person shows production of interferons in his body, the chances are that he has an infection of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tetanus, often referred to as "lockjaw," is a potentially serious bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani. The bacteria typically enter the body through cuts, wounds, or puncture injuries, and they produce a powerful neurotoxin that affects the nervous system. Preventing tetanus involves proper wound care, regular vaccination, and seeking prompt medical attention for any injury that could potentially lead to tetanus. Booster shots are recommended every ten years. Tetanus is a serious and potentially life-threatening condition, but it is highly preventable through vaccination and proper wound care. If you experience a wound that could expose you to tetanus and you're not up to date with your tetanus vaccinations, seek medical attention promptly to receive the necessary treatment and immunization.

Malaria is a parasitic infectious disease caused by the Plasmodium parasites, which are transmitted to humans through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. It is a significant global health concern, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions. Malaria is primarily transmitted through the bite of infected Anopheles mosquitoes. The parasites multiply in the mosquito's gut and are then injected into the human host's bloodstream during a mosquito bite. The choice of medication and the duration of treatment depend on the type of malaria, its severity, and the patient's age and location. Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are often used to treat uncomplicated malaria. Malaria is a preventable and treatable disease, but it remains a significant public health challenge, particularly in regions where it is endemic. Ongoing efforts to control and eventually eliminate malaria include vaccination, improved diagnostics, and access to effective treatment.

Typhoid fever, often referred to as just typhoid, is a bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi. It is a significant infectious disease that primarily affects the digestive system. Typhoid is typically spread through the consumption of contaminated food or water that contains the Salmonella Typhi bacterium. It can also be transmitted through close contact with an infected person. Typhoid fever is a serious and potentially life-threatening disease, particularly if left untreated. Adequate sanitation, clean water, vaccination, and early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for preventing and controlling typhoid. Travellers to regions where typhoid is prevalent should take precautions to reduce their risk of infection.

Measles, also known as rubeola, is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the measles virus. It primarily affects the respiratory system and is characterized by a distinctive rash. Measles is primarily transmitted through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It is highly contagious and can be easily spread in crowded or unvaccinated populations. Measles symptoms typically begin 10 to 14 days after exposure and include high fever, cough, runny nose, and red, watery eyes. A characteristic red, blotchy rash typically appears a few days later, starting on the face and spreading down the body. The measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) vaccine is highly effective at preventing measles. It is typically administered in two doses, the first around the age of one and the second around the age of four. High vaccine coverage in populations helps to control and eliminate the disease. Measles is a vaccine-preventable disease, and vaccination remains the most effective way to control its spread. Timely vaccination not only protects individuals but also contributes to herd immunity, reducing the risk of outbreaks and protecting those who cannot be vaccinated for medical reasons. If you suspect you have been exposed to measles or are showing symptoms, it's essential to seek medical attention to prevent further transmission.

questions.explanation: Interferons are antiviral proteins that increase a cell's resistance to viral attacks. Since measles is a viral disease, the body produces interferons. Measles is an acute infectious eruptive viral disease of childhood caused by the RNA-containing Rubeola virus (Polynosa morbillorum). Typhoid and tetanus are bacterial diseases caused by Salmonella typhi and Clostridium tetani, respectively. Malaria is a protozoan disease caused by Plasmodium species.

questions.text: Which of the following pairs of diseases is caused by a virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rabies is a viral disease that affects the central nervous system. It is caused by the rabies virus, which is typically transmitted through the saliva of infected animals, most commonly through the bite of an infected animal. Symptoms of rabies in humans can include fever, headache, weakness, and later, more severe symptoms like confusion, hallucinations, paralysis, and difficulty swallowing. Mumps is a viral infection caused by the mumps virus. It primarily affects the salivary glands, leading to painful swelling of the parotid glands in the cheeks, as well as other symptoms. Symptoms of mumps can include fever, headache, muscle aches, loss of appetite, and swelling of the salivary glands, which can lead to a characteristic "chipmunk cheek" appearance.

Cholera is an acute diarrheal disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae. It is typically transmitted through the ingestion of contaminated water or food. Cholera symptoms include severe diarrhea, vomiting, and leg cramps. The loss of fluids through diarrhea can lead to rapid dehydration and electrolyte imbalances. Cholera can be a severe and life-threatening disease if not treated promptly. Tuberculosis is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. It primarily affects the lungs but can also involve other parts of the body. TB symptoms can include a persistent cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss. In pulmonary TB, patients may cough up sputum containing the bacteria. TB is contagious and is spread through the air when an infected person with active TB coughs or sneezes.

Typhoid, also known as typhoid fever, is a bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi. It is primarily transmitted through the consumption of food or water contaminated with the bacterium. Symptoms of typhoid can include sustained high fever, headache, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. In severe cases, it can lead to complications such as intestinal perforation. Tetanus is a bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani. It typically enters the body through wounds, cuts, or injuries, especially those contaminated with soil or dirt. Symptoms of tetanus can include muscle stiffness, muscle spasms, and difficulty swallowing.

AIDS is a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). It primarily affects the immune system, leading to a weakened immune response and making the body more susceptible to opportunistic infections and certain cancers. Early HIV infection may not produce noticeable symptoms, but over time, without treatment, it can progress to AIDS, characterized by severe immune system impairment and the occurrence of opportunistic infections. Syphilis is a chronic sexually transmitted infection caused by Treponema pallidum. It progresses through stages—primary, secondary, latent, and tertiary—each with distinct symptoms such as painless ulcers, rashes, or systemic complications. If untreated, it can damage the heart, brain, nerves, and other organs. Early diagnosis and treatment with penicillin can completely cure the disease and prevent long-term effects.

questions.explanation: Mumps is an infectious disease that causes fever, difficulty in opening the mouth, and painful swelling of the parotid glands, which lie just below the earlobes. It is caused by a paramyxovirus, which is present in the saliva of the infected person. Rabies (hydrophobia) is caused by a virus named the rabies virus. It is introduced into the body through the bite of rabid (mad) dogs. The most important characteristic symptom of this disease is the fear of water.

questions.text: Select the wrong statement
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The term ‘contagium vivum fluidum' was coined by M. W. Beijerinck.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Martinus Beijerinck, a Dutch microbiologist, coined the term "contagium vivum fluidum" to describe infectious agents that are smaller than bacteria, which are now known as viruses.

options.text: Mosaic disease in tobacco and AIDS in human beings are caused by viruses.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Mosaic disease in tobacco is indeed caused by a virus; AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus).

options.text: The viroids were discovered by D.J. Ivanovsky.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The discovery of viroids is credited to the American pathologist Theodor O. Diener, not D.J. Ivanowsky. Viroids are small, infectious RNA molecules that are distinct from viruses.

options.text: W. M. Stanley showed that viruses could be crystallized.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Wendell Meredith Stanley, an American biochemist, successfully crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935. His work provided important evidence that viruses are distinct entities that can be purified and studied like other biological molecules.

questions.explanation: 1. "The term ‘contagium vivum fluidum' was coined by M. W. Beijerinck." - Correct. Beijerinck coined this term for infectious agents smaller than bacteria, now known as viruses.2. "Mosaic disease in tobacco and AIDS in human beings are caused by viruses." - Correct. Mosaic disease is caused by a virus, and AIDS results from HIV, a specific virus.3. "The viroids were discovered by D.J. Ivanowsky." - Incorrect. Viroids were discovered by Theodor O. Diener, not Ivanovsky.4. "W.M. Stanley showed that viruses could be crystallized." - Correct. Stanley crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935, showing viruses could be purified and studied.

questions.text: Viruses have?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: DNA is enclosed in a protein coat
options.explanation: This describes the structure of many viruses. Viruses are genetic entities that can contain either DNA or RNA as their genetic material. This genetic material is enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Some viruses also have an outer lipid envelope derived from the host cell's membrane.

Viruses do not have a nucleus, and they are not classified as prokaryotic or eukaryotic because they are not considered living organisms. Instead, they are considered obligate intracellular parasites, relying on host cells to replicate.

Viruses typically have a single chromosome (either DNA or RNA), which carries the genetic information necessary for their replication. However, the term "chromosome" is more commonly associated with the genetic material found in living cells rather than viruses.

Some viruses have DNA as their genetic material (DNA viruses), while others have RNA as their genetic material (RNA viruses). They do not contain both DNA and RNA simultaneously. The choice of DNA or RNA depends on the specific type of virus.

questions.explanation: The most accurate statement about viruses is that they contain genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat, and they lack a prokaryotic or eukaryotic nucleus. Viruses are nucleoprotein entities that can utilize the synthetic machinery of a living cell of the host organism for their multiplication, which does not involve growth and division. They have either RNA or DNA as genetic material and a protein coat.

questions.text: Which statement is wrong for viruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Viruses are classified as obligate intracellular parasites, which means they cannot reproduce independently and must invade a host cell to replicate.

options.text: All of them have helical symmetry.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Not all viruses exhibit helical symmetry; they can also have icosahedral or complex symmetrical structures. The symmetry type varies among different virus families.

options.text: They don't have the ability to synthesise nucleic acids and proteins.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Viruses lack the necessary cellular machinery to synthesize nucleic acids and proteins on their own; they rely on the host cell's mechanisms for these processes.

options.text: Antibiotics have no effect on them.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Antibiotics target bacterial infections and are ineffective against viruses, which do not possess the cellular structures that antibiotics target.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Viruses can have different symmetries, including helical, icosahedral. or complex. structures. The structure of a virus depends on its type and can vary.

questions.text: The virus envelope is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The capsid is the protein shell that encases the viral genome, providing protection and aiding in the virus's ability to infect host cells. It is crucial for the virus's structural integrity.
A virion is the complete, infective form of a virus outside of a host cell, which includes the viral genome and its protective structures, but it is not specifically the envelope.
Nucleoproteins are complexes of nucleic acids and proteins found within the virus but do not refer to the outer envelope structure.
The core of a virus typically refers to the region containing the viral RNA or DNA, but it does not encompass the outer envelope.

questions.explanation: The capsid is the protein coat that surrounds the genetic material (either DNA or RNA) of a virus. It protects the genetic material and helps the virus attach to and enter host cells. As it is outer covering in most viruses so it is the correct answer.

questions.text: Below is the diagram of a bacteriophage. In which one of the options are all four parts, A, B, C, and D, correct, respectively?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A–Head, B–Sheath, C–Collar, D–Tail fibre.
A–Head, B–Sheath, C–Collar, D–Tail fibre.
A–Head, B–Sheath, C–Collar, D–Tail fibre.
A–Head, B–Sheath, C–Collar, D–Tail fibre.

questions.explanation: A–Head, B–Sheath, C–Collar, D–Tail fibreThe head contains the genetic material of the bacteriophage, usually in the form of DNA or RNA, enclosed within a protein coat. It plays a vital role in protecting the genetic material and injecting it into the host cell during infection.The sheath is a contractile structure surrounding the tail tube that helps in injecting the phage DNA into the bacterial cell. When the phage attaches to the host, the sheath contracts to push the DNA through the bacterial membrane.The collar is a small structure that connects the head to the tail and helps in stabilizing the phage during attachment. It also assists in the transfer of genetic material from the head to the tail during infection.The tail fibres are long, thin structures that help the phage recognize and attach to specific receptor sites on the bacterial surface. They play a crucial role in determining the host range and initiating the infection process.

questions.text: T.O. Diener discovered a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Free infectious DNA was discovered by Frederick Griffith in 1928 through his experiments with Streptococcus pneumoniae. He found that harmless bacteria became virulent when exposed to heat-killed virulent cells, suggesting the presence of a “transforming principle.” Later, Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty identified this principle as DNA, proving that DNA carries genetic information and can transfer infectious traits between cells.

Prions are abnormal proteins that can convert normal proteins into their misfolded, infectious form, leading to neurodegenerative diseases such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Prusiner's research significantly advanced our understanding of prion diseases.

Bacteriophages are viruses that infect and replicate within bacterial hosts. Their discovery by d'Hérelle and Twort introduced the concept of viruses specifically targeting bacteria, leading to the study of phage biology and applications in phage therapy.

T.O. Diener is credited with the discovery of viroids in 1971 while studying the cause of the potato spindle tuber disease. Viroids are unique infectious agents composed solely of a short strand of circular, single-stranded RNA. Unlike viruses, viroids lack a protein coat and are significantly smaller in size. Despite their simplicity, they can replicate within host cells by hijacking the host's enzymatic machinery. Viroids are known to cause several plant diseases, including those affecting potatoes, citrus plants, and tomatoes.

questions.explanation: 1. Free infectious DNA—Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty (1944) indeed demonstrated that DNA is the "transforming principle," proving it carries genetic information. This was a landmark in molecular genetics. 2. Infectious protein (prion)—Stanley Prusiner (1982, Nobel Prize in 1997) introduced the term "prion" and demonstrated that certain diseases are caused by protein-only infectious agents. 3. Bacteriophage – Frederick Twort (1915) first observed a “filterable agent” that killed bacteria, and Félix d’Hérelle (1917) independently discovered and named bacteriophage. 4. Free infectious RNA—This concept is best represented by viroids (discovered by Theodor Diener in 1971), which are small circular RNAs that infect plants. Some RNA viruses also highlight RNA as infectious material, but unlike DNA, there wasn’t a single definitive early experiment attributed to just RNA.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is not true for retroviruses?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: DNA is not present at any stage in the life cycle of retroviruses
options.explanation: This statement is not true. Retroviruses have a unique life cycle that involves the conversion of their RNA genome into DNA. They carry the gene for reverse transcriptase, an enzyme that allows them to synthesize a DNA copy of their RNA genome once they infect a host cell. This DNA form is integrated into the host cell's genome.

options.text: Retroviruses carry a gene for RNA-dependent DNA polymerase
options.explanation: This statement is true. Retroviruses carry the gene for reverse transcriptase, which is also known as RNA-dependent DNA polymerase. This enzyme is responsible for transcribing the viral RNA into DNA.

options.text: The genetic material in mature retroviruses is RNA
options.explanation: This statement is true. Retroviruses store their genetic information as RNA. However, during the replication process, they reverse transcribe this RNA into DNA, which becomes integrated into the host cell's genome.

options.text: Retroviruses are causative agents for certain kinds of cancer in humans
options.explanation: This statement is true. Some retroviruses are known to be oncogenic, meaning they can cause cancer in humans. For example, the human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) is associated with the development of adult T-cell leukaemia (a form of cancer).

questions.explanation: Retroviruses are unique viruses with a complex life cycle, and their features help explain which statements are true or false. Statement (a) is false because retroviruses indeed have a DNA stage in their replication cycle. After infection, they use the enzyme reverse transcriptase to convert their RNA genome into DNA, which integrates into the host cell’s chromosomes. Statement (b) is true because retroviruses encode RNA-dependent DNA polymerase, better known as reverse transcriptase, which is essential for converting viral RNA into DNA. Statement (c) is also true since retroviruses carry RNA as their genetic material inside mature virions, which is used as a template after infection. Finally, statement (d) is true because some retroviruses, such as HTLV-1, are oncogenic, meaning they can disrupt normal host cell growth regulation and potentially cause cancers like adult T-cell leukaemia. These features distinguish retroviruses from other types of viruses.

questions.text: Viruses that infect bacteria multiply and cause their lysis, are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysozymes are enzymes that break down bacterial cell walls, but they are not viruses. They play a role in the immune response rather than causing infection.
Lipolytic refers to enzymes that break down lipids, which are fats. This term does not pertain to viruses or their interactions with bacteria.
Lytic viruses specifically infect bacteria, replicate within them, and lead to the lysis (bursting) of the bacterial cells, releasing new viral particles. These are also known as bacteriophages.

Lysogenic viruses integrate their genetic material into the bacterial host genome and can replicate without causing immediate lysis. They do not cause the host cell to burst right away, which distinguishes them from lytic viruses.

questions.explanation: Viruses that infect bacteria, multiply within them, and ultimately cause the lysis [bursting) of the bacterial host cells are called "Lytic viruses. These vinises are often. referred to as bacteriophages or phages. They are specifically designed to replicate inside bacterial hosts and lyse them, releasing new virus particles to infect other bacteria.

questions.text: Tobacco mosaic virus is a tubular filament of size?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This size is too small for the tobacco mosaic virus. A virus of this length would be more characteristic of smaller RNA viruses or roughly spherical ones rather than the long, rod-shaped structure typical of TMV.
This measurement is still shorter than that of TMV. While some filamentous plant viruses can fall within this range, the tobacco mosaic virus is distinctly longer, making this option inaccurate.

The size of TMV particles is approximately 20 nanometers (nm) in diameter and can vary in length, but they are typically around 300 nm long. These dimensions make TMV one of the largest plant viruses known.

This option incorrectly reverses the proportions. TMV is much longer than it is wide, forming a slender rod rather than a broad filament. The diameter of TMV is much smaller than 180 nm, so this option does not match its true structure.

questions.explanation: The Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) is a rod-shaped (tubular) filament measuring about 300 nanometers (nm) in length and 20 nanometers (nm) in diameter, with a central hollow core of about 4 nm.

questions.text: In what sense are viruses comparable to isolated chromosomes in terms of being “non-living”?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: They require both RNA and DNA.
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Viruses can have either RNA or DNA as their genetic material, not both. They do not require both types of nucleic acids for their replication.

Viruses do not need food molecules like living organisms do. They lack metabolic processes and do not engage in activities related to obtaining and utilizing nutrients for energy or growth. They rely on host cells for replication.

options.text: They both require oxygen for respiration.
options.explanation: Viruses do not respire, and they do not require oxygen for metabolic processes because they do not perform respiration. They do not have the necessary cellular machinery for respiration.

options.text: Both require the environment of a cell to replicate.
options.explanation: Viruses are no more "alive" than isolated chromosomes because they both require the environment of a cell to replicate. Viruses lack the cellular machinery and metabolic processes necessary to carry out the functions of living organisms on their own. Instead, they rely on infecting a host cell and hijacking its machinery to replicate and propagate.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that both isolated chromosomes and viruses require the environment of a host cell to replicate because viruses, like isolated chromosomes, cannot replicate or carry out their life cycle independently and rely on a host cell to do so. All other options are incorrect.

questions.text: Viruses possess:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Viruses do not have ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis. Instead, they hijack the ribosomes of host cells to produce their proteins.

This option is also incorrect. Viruses lack organelles, such as mitochondria or nuclei, which are characteristic of living cells. They are acellular and do not possess the complex structures necessary for metabolism or replication independent of a host.

This option is correct. Viruses contain either DNA or RNA as their genetic material, which can be either single-stranded or double-stranded. This genetic material is crucial for the virus's replication and its ability to infect host cells.
This option is incorrect. As stated in option C, viruses do have genetic material in the form of either DNA or RNA, which is fundamental to their structure and function.

questions.explanation: Viruses possess either DNA or RNA as their genetic material. This genetic material can be single-stranded or double-stranded, depending on the type of virus. The genetic material carries the instructions for the virus's replication and function.

questions.text: What is the genetic material in the influenza virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Influenza viruses do not have double-stranded DNA as their genetic material. They are classified as RNA viruses.

This is the correct answer. Influenza viruses have single-stranded RNA as their genetic material. They belong to the Orthomyxoviridae family of viruses, which are RNA viruses responsible for causing influenza in humans and other animals.
The concept of "single helix DNA" is not applicable in the context of viruses. DNA typically forms a double helix, and influenza viruses do not contain DNA as their genetic material.
This option is not correct. As explained in option B, the genetic material in the influenza virus is RNA.

questions.explanation: The genetic material in the influenza virus is single-stranded RNA. Influenza viruses are RNA viruses responsible for causing the flu in humans and other animals. This RNA serves as the blueprint for the replication and transcription of viral genes during the infection process. All other options are incorrect.

questions.text: Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) genes are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. The genes of the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) are composed of single-stranded RNA (ribonucleic acid). TMV is a plant virus known for causing the tobacco mosaic disease, which affects various plants, including tobacco, tomatoes, peppers, and other members of the Solanaceae family. The RNA genome of TMV encodes the genetic information necessary for the virus to replicate and produce its proteins during the infection process.

Double-stranded DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a genetic material found in most living organisms, including plants, animals, and bacteria. However, TMV is not a DNA virus; it is an RNA virus with a single-stranded RNA genome.

The term "proteinaceous" refers to something that is primarily composed of proteins. While TMV is a virus made up of proteins and RNA, the genes of TMV are specifically composed of single-stranded RNA, not just proteins.
This option is not correct. TMV does not have a double-stranded RNA genome. Instead, its genome consists of a single-stranded RNA molecule.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is a) single-stranded RNA. The genes of the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) are composed of single-stranded RNA. This RNA genome contains the instructions for the virus to infect host plant cells, replicate, and produce new viral particles, leading to the characteristic mosaic-like patterns and other symptoms observed in infected plants.

questions.text: Nucleic acid is the only component of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are complex infectious agents that contain nucleic acid (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protective protein coat known as a capsid. Some viruses may also have an additional lipid envelope. Because they include more than just nucleic acid, this option is not correct.

Viroids are unique infectious agents composed solely of a single strand of RNA without any protein coat. This simplicity makes viroids distinct from viruses, as they contain no other components. Thus, nucleic acid is indeed the only component of a viroid, making this the correct answer.

A virion is a complete and infectious virus particle, consisting of nucleic acid encased in a protein capsid, and sometimes an outer envelope. Given that virions always include additional structures alongside nucleic acid, this option is incorrect.

Prions are infectious agents made entirely of misfolded proteins and do not contain any nucleic acid. Since they lack nucleic acid altogether, they cannot be considered the correct answer to the question regarding the sole component being nucleic acid.

questions.explanation: Viroids are small infectious agents that consist only of a single-stranded RNA molecule. Unlike viruses, viroids do not have a protein coat (capsid) or an outer envelope. They are. simpler than viruses and contain only nucleic acid (RNA) as their genetic material. Therefore, nucleic acid is the only component of a viroid.

questions.text: It is false for viruses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Viruses contain both DNA and RNA
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Viruses do not contain both DNA and RNA simultaneously. They can have either DNA or RNA as their genetic material, but not both at the same time. A virus is classified as either a DNA virus (contains DNA) or an RNA virus (contains RNA). For example, the influenza virus is an RNA virus, while the herpes simplex virus is a DNA virus. Some viruses have single-stranded genetic material (either DNA or RNA), while others have double-stranded genetic material. The presence of either DNA or RNA is one of the defining characteristics used to classify viruses into different groups.

This statement is generally correct. Viruses lack the cellular machinery required for independent metabolic activities. Unlike living cells, viruses cannot carry out metabolic processes such as energy production, protein synthesis, or waste elimination on their own. Instead, they rely entirely on the host cells they infect to provide the necessary cellular machinery for viral replication and protein synthesis.

This statement is correct. Viruses are not cellular organisms. They are acellular entities that lack the features of living cells, such as a cell membrane, organelles, and independent metabolic activities. Instead, viruses are composed of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Some viruses may also have an outer envelope made of lipids derived from the host cell membrane.

This statement is correct. Viruses are tiny infectious agents. They are much smaller than cells and can only be observed using powerful microscopes. Despite their small size, viruses can cause a wide range of infectious diseases in humans, animals, plants, and microorganisms. When a virus infects a host cell, it can hijack the cellular machinery to produce new viral particles, leading to the spread of the infection.

questions.explanation: Viruses do not contain both DNA and RNA simultaneously. They can have either DNA or RNA as their genetic material, but not both simultaneously. A virus is classified as either a DNA virus (contains DNA) or an RNA virus (contains RNA). For example, the influenza virus is an RNA virus, while the herpes simplex virus is a DNA virus. Some viruses have single-stranded genetic material (either DNA or RNA), while others have double-stranded genetic material. The presence of either DNA or RNA is one of the defining characteristics used to classify viruses into different groups.

questions.text: The component responsible for determination of shape of virus is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nucleic acids, while essential for a virus's genetic information, do not directly determine the virus's shape. They provide the blueprint for the virus but are not structural components themselves.
The type of host a virus infects does not influence its shape. Hosts provide the environment for viral replication but do not determine viral structure.

Protein subunits are the primary structural components that assemble to form the capsid of a virus. The arrangement and type of these subunits directly influence the overall shape and structure of the virus.

While the viral envelope can affect the virus's appearance and its ability to infect hosts, it does not determine the virus's fundamental shape, which is primarily dictated by the capsid formed from protein subunits.

questions.explanation: A protein subunit is a polypeptide chain or single protein molecule that assembles (or "coassembles") with others to form a protein complex. Large assemblies of proteins such as viruses often use a small number of types of protein subunits as building blocks.

questions.text: In prions, information is carried further through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prion DNA does not exist. Prions are infectious agents that are composed of misfolded proteins. They do not contain any nucleic acids, such as DNA or RNA. This makes prions unique among infectious agents.

The prion protein is a normal protein that is found in the brain of all mammals. It is thought to play a role in cell-to-cell signaling. However, when the prion protein misfolds, it becomes a prion. Prions are very stable proteins and are not easily destroyed by the body's immune system.

Prion DNA does not exist. Prions are infectious agents that are composed of misfolded proteins. They do not contain any nucleic acids, such as DNA or RNA. This makes prions unique among infectious agents.

Glycoproteins are proteins that have carbohydrate (sugar) chains covalently attached to them. They are commonly found on the surface of cells and viruses, where they play key roles in cell–cell recognition, signalling, immune responses, and as receptors for pathogens. In viruses, glycoproteins help in attachment and entry into host cells, while in humans, they are important components of cell membranes, antibodies, and hormones.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Prions are misfolded or infectious proteins with the ability to transmit their misfolded shape onto normal variants of the same protein, and information is carried through the conformational change of the prion protein. Prions don't contain DNA or RNA, as they are chemically infectious proteins. Glycoproteins are involved in attachment and entry into host cells.

questions.text: _ capsomeres are present in the capsid of the herpes virus.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The herpes virus has 162 capsomeres. The given information is incorrect. The polio virus contains 32 capsomeres.
The herpes virus has 162 capsomeres. The given information is incorrect. Adenovirus contains 252 capsomeres.
The herpes virus has 162 capsomeres. The given information is correct.
The herpes virus has 162 capsomeres. The given information is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. The capsid of viruses is made up of protein subunits known as capsomeres. The number of capsomeres is characteristic of a particular virus. i.e., the herpes virus has 162 capsomeres, the adenovirus has 252 capsomeres, and polio has 32 capsomeres in its capsid.

questions.text: The phenomenon of transcription in the HIV life cycle occurs in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleus of the host cell is where transcription of the HIV genome takes place after the viral DNA has integrated into the host’s chromosomes. Once reverse transcription converts the viral RNA into DNA, this DNA enters the nucleus and integrates with the host DNA. The host’s RNA polymerase II enzyme then transcribes the integrated proviral DNA into viral mRNA, which is later transported to the cytoplasm for translation and assembly of new viral particles.

While the cytoplasm is critical for various stages of the HIV life cycle, such as reverse transcription and translation of proteins, it is not the site where transcription of the HIV genome occurs. The viral RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA in the cytoplasm, but the actual transcription into RNA occurs in the nucleus, where the host's transcription machinery is located.

The viral capsid serves as a protective shell for the viral RNA and essential enzymes necessary for early infection stages. However, it lacks the cellular machinery required for RNA synthesis, making transcription impossible within the capsid. The transcription process must occur in the nucleus after the viral DNA integrates into the host genome.

This option is incorrect as the nucleus is indeed the correct location for the transcription of the HIV genome after integration. Therefore, saying 'none of these' fails to recognize the accurate role of the nucleus in the HIV life cycle.

questions.explanation: In the nucleus, Reverse transcription occurs, the viral RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the reverse transcriptase enzymeIntegration: The DNA is integrated into the DNA of the host cell by the integrase enzyme. Transcription: The integrated DNA is transcribed into RNA by the host cell's DNA polymerase.

questions.text: The major cell infected by HIV is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A leukocyte is a type of white blood cell that is involved in the immune system. There are five main types of leukocytes: neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, monocytes, and lymphocytes. These are the types of white blood cells and helper T-lymphocytes are one of the cells that are affected by HIV. So, this option is not correct.

A monocyte is a type of leukocyte that is derived from bone marrow and matures in the blood. Monocytes are phagocytic cells, which means that they can engulf and destroy foreign invaders. Monocytes also produce cytokines, which are proteins that help to regulate the immune response. HIV affects helper T-Lymphocytes and causes AIDS, so this answer is incorrect.

A helper T-lymphocyte is a type of lymphocyte that helps to coordinate the immune response. Helper T-lymphocytes produce cytokines that activate other immune cells, such as B-lymphocytes and cytotoxic T-lymphocytes. These cells are affected by HIV, which causes AIDS.

A B-lymphocyte is a type of lymphocyte that produces antibodies. Antibodies are proteins that bind to specific antigens, which are foreign substances that the body recognizes as being harmful. B-lymphocytes are activated by helper T-lymphocytes, and they then produce antibodies that help to fight infection. HIV affects helper T-lymphocytes and causes AIDS, so this answer is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is helper T-lymphocytes (option C). Leukocytes are white blood cells involved in immunity, including neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, monocytes, and lymphocytes. Monocytes are phagocytic cells that engulf pathogens and produce cytokines. B-lymphocytes produce antibodies when activated by helper T-lymphocytes. Helper T-lymphocytes coordinate the immune response by producing cytokines that activate other immune cells, and HIV specifically targets these cells, leading to AIDS.

questions.text: Chemically, viruses are made up of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because while viruses contain nucleic acid, they also require proteins to form the capsid that protects this genetic material. Viruses can have either DNA or RNA, but they cannot solely consist of nucleic acid.

This option is also incorrect. Although proteins are essential for the structure of viruses, they cannot exist without nucleic acids. Viruses are made up of both nucleic acids and proteins, making this option incomplete.

This is the correct option. Viruses are composed of a nucleic acid core, which can be either DNA or RNA, and a protein coat called the capsid that encases the nucleic acid. This combination is essential for viral structure and function.

This option is misleading. While some viruses possess a lipid envelope derived from the host's cell membrane, lipoproteins are not a universal component of all viruses. The fundamental structure of all viruses is nucleic acid and protein.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option C, which states that viruses are made up of both nucleic acid and protein. This dual composition is crucial for their functionality. The nucleic acid, which can be either DNA or RNA, houses the genetic information necessary for the virus to replicate and infect host cells. The protein components, primarily the capsid, protect the nucleic acid and facilitate the virus's interaction with host cells.Options A and B are incorrect as they only consider one component of viral structure, neglecting the essential presence of the other. Option D is misleading because while some viruses do have a lipid envelope, it is not a characteristic applicable to all viruses, making nucleic acid and protein the fundamental components.

questions.text: Reverse transcriptase is used to make DNA copies of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Host RNA is not used as a template by reverse transcriptase.

Reverse transcriptase is used to make DNA copies of viral RNA. Reverse transcriptase is an enzyme that is found in retroviruses, which are a type of virus that carries its genetic material in the form of RNA. The reverse transcriptase enzyme uses the viral RNA as a template to synthesize a DNA copy. This DNA copy is then integrated into the DNA of the host cell, where it can be replicated and transcribed into new viral RNA.
Host DNA is not used as a template by reverse transcriptase.
Reverse transcriptase uses viral RNA, not viral DNA, as a template.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option B. HIV carries reverse transcriptase, which converts its viral RNA genome into DNA in infected cells. The process involves three steps: initiation, where the enzyme binds viral RNA and a primer; elongation, where a DNA copy of the RNA is synthesized; and termination, when synthesis ends at a specific sequence. Host RNA or DNA is not used as a template—only the viral RNA serves for reverse transcription.

questions.text: Which of the following cells are mainly infected by HIV?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T-killer lymphocytes, also known as cytotoxic T-cells, are a type of T-cell that is responsible for killing infected cells. They are a part of the adaptive immune system and play a role in cell-mediated immunity. T-killer lymphocytes are derived from T-cells that mature in the thymus gland. In the thymus, T-cells are exposed to a variety of antigens, which helps them to develop the ability to recognize and respond to specific invaders. HIV attacks on helper T-lymphocytes so this option is incorrect.

Helper T-lymphocytes, also known as CD4+ T-cells, are a type of T-cell that helps other immune cells to fight infection. They are a part of the adaptive immune system and play a role in cell-mediated immunity. Helper T-lymphocytes are derived from T-cells that mature in the thymus gland. In the thymus, T-cells are exposed to a variety of antigens, which helps them to develop the ability to recognize and respond to specific invaders. This is the correct answer, as HIV attacks these cells of the immune system.

B-plasma cells are mature B-cells that produce antibodies. They are found in the bone marrow and lymph nodes. B-plasma cells are responsible for the humoral immune response, which is the part of the immune system that fights infection using antibodies. HIV attacks helper T-lymphocytes, so this option is incorrect.

B-memory cells are a type of B-cell that is produced during the primary immune response. They are long-lived cells that can survive for many years. B-memory cells are important for the secondary immune response, which is the faster and more robust immune response that occurs when a person is exposed to an antigen that they have been exposed to before. HIV attacks helper T-lymphocytes, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option B—helper T-lymphocytes (CD4+ T-cells). These cells, derived from T-cells maturing in the thymus, coordinate the immune response by activating other immune cells. T-killer (cytotoxic) T-cells destroy infected cells, while B-plasma cells produce antibodies, and B-memory cells provide long-term immunity. HIV specifically targets helper T-lymphocytes, impairing immune coordination, which makes the other options incorrect.

questions.text: HIV is classified as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A bacteriophage, also known as a phage, is a virus that infects and replicates within bacteria. Phages are typically much simpler than other viruses, consisting of only a protein coat and a nucleic acid genome. The genome of a bacteriophage can be either DNA or RNA, and it can be single-stranded or double-stranded. This is incorrect.

An oncovirus is a virus that can cause cancer. Oncoviruses are a type of retrovirus, which means that they carry their genetic material in the form of RNA. The RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the virus's reverse transcriptase enzyme. The DNA is then integrated into the DNA of the host cell, where it can be replicated and transcribed into new viral RNA. It is not the HIV virus, so it is not correct.

Retroviruses are a type of virus that uses RNA as its genetic material. After infecting their host cells, the retrovirus uses an enzyme called reverse transcriptase to convert its genomic RNA into DNA. The retrovirus then integrates its viral DNA into the DNA of the host cell, which allows the retrovirus to replicate. One typical example of a retrovirus is HIV. So, this option is the correct one.

An icosahedral virus is a virus that has a protein shell that is made up of 20 triangular faces. The icosahedral shape is the most common shape for viruses, and it is found in a wide variety of viruses, including poliovirus, rhinovirus, and adenovirus. It is not HIV virus so it is not correct.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option C - retrovirus. Retroviruses are RNA viruses that use reverse transcriptase to convert their RNA genome into DNA after infecting a host cell. This viral DNA is then integrated into the host genome, allowing the virus to replicate. HIV is a typical example of a retrovirus. Other options are incorrect: bacteriophages infect bacteria, oncoviruses cause cancer but are not necessarily HIV, and icosahedral viruses are defined by their protein shell shape and do not specifically describe HIV.

questions.text: AIDS is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AIDS is not caused by bacteria. It is caused by a virus called the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it carries its genetic material in the form of RNA.

AIDS is caused by a virus. The virus is called the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it carries its genetic material in the form of RNA. The RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the virus's reverse transcriptase enzyme. The DNA is then integrated into the DNA of the host cell, where it can be replicated and transcribed into new viral RNA.

AIDS is not caused by fungi. Fungi are a different type of pathogen than viruses. Fungi are single-celled or multicellular organisms that can cause a variety of diseases, including athlete's foot, ringworm, and candidiasis. However, fungi do not cause AIDS.
AIDS is not caused by algae. Algae are a type of plant that can cause a variety of health problems, but they do not cause AIDS.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Yes, AIDS is caused by a virus. The virus is called the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is a retrovirus, which means that it carries its genetic material in the form of RNA. The RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA by the virus's reverse transcriptase enzyme. The DNA is then integrated into the DNA of the host cell, where it can be replicated and transcribed into new viral RNA. It is not caused by bacteria, fungi, or algae.

questions.text: Cilia and flagella are absent in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are acellular entities, meaning they lack cellular structures. They consist of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat (capsid). Since they are not cells, they do not have cilia or flagella. Viruses rely on the host cell's machinery for their replication and movement.

Bacteria are unicellular prokaryotic organisms. While some bacteria can have various structures for locomotion, such as flagella, not all of them have cilia or flagella. Some bacteria move by other means, like twitching, gliding, or using pili, depending on their specific structures. As some bacteria have these structures, this option cannot be considered correct.

Many protists, which are eukaryotic microorganisms, have cilia or flagella. These structures are commonly used for locomotion and feeding in protists. However, there are exceptions, and not all protists have cilia or flagella. Some may use other structures for movement, such as pseudopods. As some may have these structures, this option is not correct.

Cilia and flagella are present in some lower animals, but not all of them. Cilia are hair-like structures that project from the surface of cells, and they help to move the cell or move fluids around the cell. Flagella are longer and whip-like structures that also project from the cell surface, and they help the cell to move. As some may have these structures, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The presence of cilia and flagella varies among different organisms. While they are generally absent in viruses, their presence or absence in bacteria, protists, and lower animals can vary depending on the specific organism and its evolutionary adaptations.

questions.text: In viruses, a combined structure formed by the core (nucleic acid) and the capsid is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A nucleocapsid is the protein coat that surrounds the nucleic acid of a virus particle. It is made up of one or more types of proteins that are specifically encoded by the virus genome. The nucleocapsid protects the nucleic acid from damage and helps to direct the replication of the virus. It is made of nucleic acid (genome) and a protein coat (capsid). It is the correct option.

A virus envelope is the outermost layer of some viruses. It is made up of a lipid bilayer that is derived from the host cell membrane. The envelope contains viral proteins that help the virus attach to and enter host cells. This option is incorrect.

A capsomere is a protein subunit that makes up the capsid, an outer covering of protein that protects the genetic material of a virus. Capsomeres self-assemble to form the capsid. The capsomeres protect against physical, chemical, and enzymatic damage and are multiply redundant, having a few protein subunits that are repeated. This option is incorrect.

Prions are infectious agents that are composed of misfolded proteins. Prions are not like other infectious agents, such as viruses or bacteria, because they do not contain any nucleic acids. Instead, they are simply misfolded proteins that can induce other proteins to fold abnormally. This can lead to the accumulation of misfolded proteins in the brain, which can cause damage and eventually death. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. A nucleocapsid is the complex of viral nucleic acid (genome) and protein coat (capsid) that protects the genome and directs replication. Option B, the viral envelope, is a lipid bilayer derived from the host cell membrane and not the nucleocapsid. Option C, a capsomere, is just a protein subunit of the capsid, not the entire nucleocapsid. Option D, prions, are infectious proteins without nucleic acids and thus unrelated to nucleocapsids.

questions.text: The capsid, the protective coat of a virus, is made up of _ subunits known as capsomeres.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the capsid is not made of lipid. The capsid is made up of protein subunits called capsomeres. The capsomeres are arranged in a regular geometric pattern to form the capsid. This option is incorrect.

Chemically, a virion is made up of a core of genetic material: either DNA or RNA. Surrounded by a protective coat called a capsid, which is made up of protein. The capsid of viruses is made up of protein subunits known as capsomeres. The number of capsomeres is characteristic of a particular virus. So, it is the correct option.

The capsid is not made of RNA. It is made up of protein subunits called capsomeres. RNA is the genetic material of some viruses. It is enclosed in the capsid, but it is not part of the capsid itself. The RNA is responsible for encoding the proteins that make up the capsid. This option is incorrect.

The capsid is not made of DNA. DNA is the genetic material of some viruses. It is enclosed in the capsid, but it is not part of the capsid itself. The DNA is responsible for encoding the proteins that make up the capsid.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Chemically, a virion is made up of a core of genetic material: either DNA or RNA surrounded by a protective coat called a capsid, which is made up of protein. The capsid of viruses is made up of protein subunits known as capsomeres. The number of capsomeres is characteristic of a particular virus. The capsid is not made up of lipids, RNA and DNA.

questions.text: _ enzyme is not found in human immunodeficiency virus.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA polymerase is crucial for HIV as it enables the virus to convert its RNA into DNA after it enters a host cell. This is a key step in the viral replication cycle, making DNA polymerase present in HIV.
Reverse transcriptase is essential for HIV, as it allows the virus to transcribe its RNA genome into DNA. This process is fundamental for the integration of the viral genome into the host cell's DNA.
RNA polymerase synthesizes RNA from a DNA template, a process that HIV does not require since it operates using its own RNA. Thus, HIV does not have RNA polymerase.

Ligase is involved in joining DNA fragments together, which is important for DNA replication and repair. While HIV uses ligase during its lifecycle, it is not one of the key enzymes that define HIV's unique replication process as a retrovirus.

questions.explanation: RNA polymerase is an enzyme that is used by cells to make RNA from DNA. It is not found in HIV, because HIV does not need to make RNA from DNA. Instead, it uses the RNA that it already has to make new copies of the virus.

questions.text: The filterable agents were first purified in 1935, when Stanley was successful in crystallizing the _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Karl Landsteiner and Erwin Popper discovered the poliovirus in 1908. Landsteiner was an Austrian-American pathologist and immunologist, while Popper was an Austrian physician. They isolated the virus from the spinal cord of a child who had died of poliomyelitis.

Stanley was the first scientist to crystallize the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935. TMV is transmitted from infected plants to healthy plants through contact, such as by touching an infected leaf or by using the same tools on both infected and healthy plants. TMV can also be transmitted through the air, but this is less common.

Baruch Blumberg, who discovered the hepatitis B virus (HBV) in 1967. Blumberg was an Australian-American physician and immunologist who was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1976 for his discovery.

Wilson Smith, Christopher H. Andrewes, and Patrick P. Laidlaw are credited with the discovery of the influenza virus in 1933. They were working at the National Institute for Medical Research in London, England, when they isolated the virus from the throat washings of a patient with influenza.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The poliovirus was discovered in 1908 by Karl Landsteiner and Erwin Popper, who isolated it from the spinal cord of a child who died of poliomyelitis. In 1935, Wendell Stanley first crystallized the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), which spreads mainly through contact between infected and healthy plants. The hepatitis B virus (HBV) was discovered by Baruch Blumberg in 1967, earning him the Nobel Prize in 1976. The influenza virus was identified in 1933 by Wilson Smith, Christopher H. Andrewes, and Patrick P. Laidlaw at the National Institute for Medical Research in London.

questions.text: Tobacco mosaic virus was discovered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stanley was the first scientist to crystallise the tobacco mosaic virus in 1935.TMV is transmitted from infected plants to healthy plants through contact, such as by touching an infected leaf or by using the same tools on both infected and healthy plants. TMV can also be transmitted through the air, but this is less common.

Louis Pasteur was a French chemist and microbiologist who is considered the father of microbiology. He made many important discoveries in the field of microbiology, including the germ theory of disease, pasteurization, and vaccines against rabies and anthrax.

Ivanowsky was a Russian scientist who worked on the tobacco mosaic virus. He ground diseased leaves of tobacco plants and filtered them using a porcelain filter. He put this filtrate on healthy leaves and they got a disease. This showed tobacco plants were not infected by bacteria but by viruses.

Félix D'Hérelle was a French-Canadian microbiologist who is credited with the discovery of bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria. In 1917, he observed that certain bacterial cultures would clear up spontaneously, and he hypothesised that a virus was responsible for the clearing. He named the virus a bacteriophage, from the Greek words for "bacteria" and "eater."

questions.explanation: Stanley was the first scientist to crystallize the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) in 1935. TMV spreads mainly through contact between infected and healthy plants or contaminated tools. Ivanowsky, a Russian scientist, discovered that TMV could pass through filters that trapped bacteria, proving it was caused by a virus. Félix D’Hérelle discovered bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria, in 1917. Louis Pasteur, the father of microbiology, developed the germ theory of disease and created vaccines for rabies and anthrax.

questions.text: Animal viruses cannot replicate in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Blood plasma is a fluid component of blood that does not contain living cells, making it an unsuitable environment for viral replication.
While some animal viruses can infect skin cells, there are various types of skin cells, and not all viruses can replicate in every type of skin cell.
Plant cells are fundamentally different from animal cells, and viruses that infect animals typically cannot replicate in plant cells.
This option correctly states that animal viruses cannot replicate in blood plasma, skin cells, or plant cells, as they require specific living host cells.

questions.explanation: Animal viruses cannot replicate in blood plasma because it is not made of living celis. They also cannot replicate in plant cells since plant cells very different from animal cells. Even in skin cells, viruses may not replicate without the right conditions. Therefore, they cannot replicate in any of these.

questions.text: Members of which of the following groups are all parasites?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All viruses are parasites. They are obligate intracellular parasites, which means that they can only replicate inside the cells of a living host. Viruses do not have their own metabolism or ribosomes, so they need to hijack the host cell's machinery to reproduce.

There are no fungi groups that are all parasites. Some fungi are parasitic, but others are not. Parasitic fungi are those that live on or in other organisms and derive nutrients from them. They can cause diseases in plants, animals, and humans.

Not all algae are parasites. Some algae are free-living, meaning they live on their own and do not need to attach to or take nutrients from other organisms. However, some algae are parasitic, meaning they attach to other organisms and derive nutrients from them.

Bacteria are not parasites. Parasites are organisms that live on or in other organisms and derive nutrients from them. Bacteria, on the other hand, are single-celled organisms that can live independently or in colonies. They can be found in a variety of environments, including the human body, the soil, and the water.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. All viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they can only replicate inside living host cells because they lack metabolism and ribosomes. In contrast, not all fungi or algae are parasitic—some species are free-living, while others may cause disease. Bacteria are also not strictly parasites; they are independent single-celled organisms that can live freely or in association with hosts. Thus, viruses are the best example of true obligate parasites.

questions.text: The volume of bacteriophage is about _ of the host.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The volume of a bacteriophage is about 1/1000th the volume of its host.
The volume of a bacteriophage is about 1/1000th the volume of its host.
The volume of a bacteriophage is about 1/1000th the volume of its host.
The volume of a bacteriophage is about 1/1000th the volume of its host.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. Viruses are very small. The size of the largest virus (Poxvirus) is almost equal to the size of the smallest bacteria. The volume of a bacteriophage is about 1/1000th the volume of its host.

questions.text: It is true for viruses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses contain either DNA or RNA as their genetic material. They do not contain both DNA and RNA.
Viruses do not have a cell structure. They are simply packages of nucleic acids and proteins.

Yes, viruses are both tiny and infectious agents. Viruses are the smallest infectious agents known to exist. They are so small that they can only be seen with an electron microscope. Viruses are not cells, but they are made up of nucleic acids (DNA or RNA) and proteins. They cannot reproduce on their own, but they can hijack the cells of a living host to reproduce.
Viruses do not have a metabolism. They cannot produce their own energy or synthesize their own proteins.

questions.explanation: Viruses are tiny infectious agents that lack many of the key characteristics of living things. They cannot reproduce on their own, as they must infect a host cell to multiply. They have no metabolism and cannot produce energy or synthesize proteins. Unlike cells, viruses lack cellular structure and consist only of nucleic acids (either DNA or RNA, never both) enclosed in a protein coat. They are the smallest infectious agents, visible only under an electron microscope, and rely entirely on host cells for replication.

questions.text: Viruses cannot be grown on artificial culture media because they are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, viruses are not facultative parasites. Facultative parasites are organisms that can live as either parasites or free-living organisms.
Endoparasites are organisms that live inside the cells of their hosts. Viruses are not able to live on their own. They need to infect a host cell to replicate.
Obligate intracellular parasites are organisms that can only live and reproduce inside the cells of a host organism. They are unable to live or reproduce on their own.
Ectoparasites are organisms that live on the outside of their hosts, and viruses can't be endoparasites, as they are dependent upon hosts for their survival.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Viruses cannot be grown in artificial media because they lack the metabolic machinery to synthesize their own nucleic acids and proteins. They are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they can only live and reproduce inside host cells, unlike facultative parasites that can survive freely or endoparasitically, and ectoparasites that live on the surface of hosts.

questions.text: Cilia and flagella are absent in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are acellular entities, meaning they lack cellular structures. They consist of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat (capsid). Since they are not cells, they do not have cilia or flagella. Viruses rely on the host cell's machinery for their replication and movement. So, it is the correct option.

Many protists, which are eukaryotic microorganisms, have cilia or flagella. These structures are commonly used for locomotion and feeding in protists. However, there are exceptions, and not all protists have cilia or flagella. Some may use other structures for movement, such as pseudopods. As some may have these structures, this option is not correct.

Cilia and flagella are absent in bacteria. Bacteria are prokaryotes, which means they do not have a nucleus or other membrane-bound organelles. Cilia and flagella are both eukaryotic structures, so they are not found in bacteria. As some bacteria have these structures, this option cannot be considered correct.

Cilia and flagella are present in some lower animals, but not all of them. Cilia are hair-like structures that project from the surface of cells, and they help to move the cell or move fluids around the cell. Flagella are longer, whip-like structures that also project from the cell surface, and they help the cell to move through fluids. As some may have these structures, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. Viruses are acellular, consisting only of genetic material (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat, and lack cilia or flagella. They depend entirely on host cells for replication and movement. In contrast, some bacteria, protists, and lower animals may possess cilia or flagella for locomotion or feeding, but since not all do, these options are not correct.

questions.text: Viruses can only survive and reproduce inside a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses can only reproduce inside cells, but not necessarily animal cells. Some viruses can only infect plant cells, while others can infect bacterial cells.
Viruses can survive and reproduce inside bacterial cells. Viruses can infect a wide variety of cells, including animal cells, plant cells, and bacterial cells.
Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning that they can only survive and reproduce inside a living cell.

No, viruses cannot survive and reproduce inside non-living cells. Viruses are not alive, and they need a living cell to replicate. When a virus infects a cell, it injects its genetic material into the cell. The cell's machinery then uses the genetic material to produce new copies of the virus. These new copies of the virus can then infect other cells, and the cycle continues.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites that can only reproduce inside living cells, whether plant, animal, or bacterial. They are not considered living organisms since they cannot replicate on their own or survive in non-living cells. Instead, they inject their genetic material into a host cell and use its machinery to produce new viruses, which then go on to infect other cells.

questions.text: HIV is classified as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A bacteriophage, also known as a phage, is a virus that infects and replicates within bacteria. Phages are typically much simpler than other viruses, consisting of only a protein coat and a nucleic acid genome. The genome of a bacteriophage can be either DNA or RNA, and it can be single-stranded or double-stranded.

Retroviruses are a type of virus that uses RNA as its genetic material. After infecting their host cells, the retrovirus uses an enzyme called reverse transcriptase to convert its genomic RNA into DNA. The retrovirus then integrates its viral DNA into the DNA of the host cell, which allows the retrovirus to replicate. One typical example of a retrovirus is HIV.
Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver. It can be caused by a virus, bacteria, or other factors. The most common types of hepatitis are caused by viruses: hepatitis A, B, C, D, and E.

An icosahedral virus is a virus that has a protein shell that is made up of 20 triangular faces. The icosahedral shape is the most common shape for viruses, and it is found in a wide variety of viruses, including poliovirus, rhinovirus, and adenovirus.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Retroviruses use RNA as their genetic material and employ reverse transcriptase to form DNA, which integrates into the host genome for replication. HIV is a classic example. In contrast, bacteriophages infect bacteria, hepatitis is liver inflammation often caused by viruses (A–E), and icosahedral viruses have a capsid with 20 triangular faces, as seen in poliovirus and adenovirus.

questions.text: The genome of the influenza virus is made up of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Influenza is an RNA enveloped virus. It contains a single-stranded RNA.
Double-stranded DNA viruses: herpesvirus, papillomavirus, and poxvirus. Thus, this option is incorrect, as influenza is a single-stranded RNA virus.
Single-stranded DNA viruses: parvovirus, circovirus. Thus, this option is incorrect, as influenza is a single-stranded RNA virus.
Double-stranded RNA viruses: reovirus, rotavirus. Thus, this option is incorrect, as influenza is a single-stranded RNA virus.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct.Influenza is an RNA enveloped virus. It contains a single standard RNA.Viruses are classified on different bases, such as capsid shape, types of hosts, ts, and genome. Based on the genome, there are categories of viruses such as• Double-stranded viruses: herpesviruses, papillomaviruses, poxvirusesSingle-stranded viruses:parvovirus, circovirus • Double-stranded viruses:Reovirus, rotavirus • Single-stranded viruses: poliovirus, coronavirus, Influenza virus, HIV

questions.text: TMV are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: There are lambda phages that are shaped like tadpoles.
Tobacco mosaic viruses (TMV) are rod-shaped or helical-shaped viruses.
There are no viruses that are shaped like springs.
Polio viruses are spherical in shape.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.Common shapes of viruses:• Spherical (Polio)• Rod-like or helical (TMV)• Tadpole (Lambda phages)• Bacteriophage (Polyhedral)
