This is because they show both characteristics: they behave like non-living matter outside a host cell (no metabolism, no cellular structure), but once inside a host, they replicate, mutate, and evolve like living organisms.

The word "threshold" implies being at the boundary or edge of life. While this seems close, it is less commonly used in biology to describe viruses. "Transitional" is the more standard term used in textbooks and scientific explanations.
This option would only be correct if none of the given terms applied to viruses. However, since "transitional" accurately describes their dual nature, this option is not the right choice.

questions.explanation: Among the options, the most accurate term is “transitional,” because viruses display features of both living and non-living entities: they are inert and crystalline outside the host but become active and reproduce inside it. The other terms ("balanced," "threshold," and "none of these") are less precise or incorrect in describing their unique status.

questions.text: When a virus attacks an unfamiliar organism, it mutates many times and comes as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When a virus enters an unfamiliar host, it may undergo mutations that allow it to overcome the host’s immune defenses. In some cases, this can make the virus more virulent (causing more severe disease) and more dangerous for the new host.

When adapting to a new host, viruses typically accumulate more mutations as they try different genetic variations to survive. Some of these mutations are beneficial (helping transmission or virulence), while many others may be neutral or harmful to the virus.

Mutations can also help a virus spread more easily between individuals. For example, changes in surface proteins may improve binding to host receptors, making the virus more transmissible without necessarily increasing disease severity.

This option recognizes that mutations can sometimes result in greater virulence, higher transmissibility, and increased variation at the same time. However, this is not always guaranteed—some mutations may weaken the virus instead.

questions.explanation: When a virus attacks an unfamiliar organism, it undergoes repeated mutations. These mutations can lead to the virus becoming more virulent, more transmissible, or simply more mutated overall. Therefore, the most comprehensive answer is “All of these,” since viruses can develop multiple new properties during adaptation to a new host.

questions.text: Viruses are classified into many groups based on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are classified based on the type of nucleic acid they possess: DNA or RNA, single-stranded or double-stranded, linear or circular, and segmented or non-segmented.Example: Retroviruses (RNA genome), Adenoviruses (DNA genome).
Viral classification considers capsid symmetry, such as icosahedral, helical, or complex structures.Example: Tobacco Mosaic Virus (helical), Adenovirus (icosahedral).
Viruses are also classified based on the host they infect:Animal viruses (e.g., Rabies virus)Plant viruses (e.g., Tobacco Mosaic Virus)Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria)
Since nucleic acid type, capsid symmetry, and host range are all valid classification features, this option is the most inclusive and correct answer.

questions.explanation: Viruses are extremely diverse and do not fit into the typical classification systems used for cellular organisms. To properly study and identify them, scientists classify viruses based on several important characteristics:Nucleic acid type (DNA or RNA, single-stranded or double-stranded) helps determine their replication strategies and genetic properties.Capsid symmetry (helical, icosahedral, or complex) gives insight into their structure and stability.Host cell infectivity distinguishes viruses based on the organisms they infect, such as bacteria (bacteriophages), plants (plant viruses), or animals/humans (animal viruses).

questions.text: Viruses use which of the following enzymes for the breakdown of the bacterial cell wall?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteriophage contains the genes for the lysozyme enzyme. During the penetration phase, the bacteriophage's tail secretes the enzyme lysozyme, which breaks down the bacterial cell wall into segments.

Lipase is an enzyme that breaks down triglycerides into free fatty acids and glycerol by catalyzing the hydrolysis of the ester bonds in triglycerides. [5] Lipases are present in pancreatic secretions and participate in fat digestion and metabolism.

Proteases are the single class of enzymes that occupy a pivotal position with respect to their applications in both physiological and commercial fields. Proteolytic enzymes catalyze the cleavage of peptide bonds in other proteins. Proteases are degradative enzymes which catalyse the total hydrolysis of proteins.
Nucleases are enzymes that degrade nucleic acids, either DNA or RNA. DNases degrade DNA, and RNases degrade RNA.

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages use lysozyme to destroy the bacterial cell wall because peptidoglycan is made of alternating NAG–NAM sugars linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bonds, and lysozyme specifically hydrolyzes these bonds, causing lysis. Lipases, proteases, and nucleases are ineffective since they act on lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids, respectively, which are not the main structural components of the bacterial wall. For example, just as a key is designed to open a specific lock, lysozyme is the precise “key” for breaking peptidoglycan, while the other enzymes do not fit this purpose.

questions.text: Viral proteins and the genome of enveloped viruses in the host cell are assembled at:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cell membrane is the primary site of assembly and release for many enveloped viruses, such as the influenza virus, HIV, and rabies virus. During this process, the viral nucleocapsid moves to the inner surface of the cell membrane, where viral envelope proteins have been inserted. The virus then buds through the membrane, taking part of the lipid bilayer as its envelope. This site allows the virus to be assembled and released simultaneously, making it the correct answer for most enveloped viruses.

Some viral envelope glycoproteins are indeed synthesized, glycosylated, and processed in the ER, but complete assembly of virions does not occur in the ER lumen. The ER is a processing station, not the final packaging site for most viruses.

Ribosomes are only responsible for protein synthesis, not for the assembly of viral proteins with the genome.After proteins are made, they must be transported to the appropriate site where genome packaging occurs.Thus, ribosomes are involved in synthesis but not in final viral assembly.
Mitochondria are not involved in viral assembly.Viruses rely on host energy (ATP) from mitochondria, but assembly of proteins and genomes into virions never takes place here.

questions.explanation: Among the given options, the correct site of viral assembly is the plasma membrane or the site of viral replication within the host cell. Ribosomes are important for synthesizing viral proteins but do not carry out assembly. The endoplasmic reticulum lumen plays a role in processing and glycosylating viral membrane proteins but is not the final assembly site. Mitochondria provide energy to the cell but are not directly involved in viral replication or assembly. Therefore, only Option A correctly identifies the site where viral proteins and the genome come together to form complete viral particles.

questions.text: Viruses can bud from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria are the energy-producing organelles of the cell. Viruses do not bud from mitochondrial membranes because mitochondria are not part of the secretory or transport pathways of the cell. While viruses may alter mitochondrial function to benefit replication, there is no evidence of viral budding occurring from mitochondria.

Ribosomes are responsible for protein synthesis, not for budding or membrane envelopment. Viral proteins are made on host ribosomes, but ribosomes themselves are not membrane structures and cannot provide the lipid bilayer needed for viral envelopes. Thus, viruses do not bud from ribosomes.

Some viruses may pass through or utilize lysosomal pathways for exit, but budding does not occur from lysosomal membranes. Budding requires insertion of viral glycoproteins into a membrane that is accessible for release, such as the plasma membrane or ER/Golgi membranes. Lysosomes mainly function in degradation, making them unsuitable sites for budding.

Enveloped viruses mature by budding at cellular membranes. It has been generally thought that this process is driven by interactions between the viral transmembrane proteins and the internal virion components (core, capsid, or nucleocapsid).

questions.explanation: Viruses acquire their envelopes by budding through host cell membranes, with the plasma membrane being the primary site for many enveloped viruses like HIV and influenza. This allows the virus to incorporate host-derived lipids along with its own viral proteins into the envelope. In contrast, mitochondria are metabolic organelles and not used for budding, ribosomes lack membranes and only make proteins, and lysosomes are degradative organelles rather than exit sites. Therefore, the correct answer is that viruses form buds from the plasma membrane, while the other options are incorrect.

questions.text: _ refers to the breakdown or removal of the capsid.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Assembly refers to the process by which new virus particles are assembled and packaged with viral genomes and proteins in the host cell.

The architecture of a virus particle allows the timely release of the viral genome in a host cell during entry. This critical step is known as viral uncoating. It is regulated by cues from receptors, enzymes, and chemicals and facilitated by factors that do not contact the virion directly.
Integration refers to the process by which a viral genome becomes integrated into the host cell's genome, typically in the case of retroviruses.
Maturation refers to the process by which newly assembled virus particles undergo structural changes to become infectious, including the acquisition of an envelope in enveloped viruses.

questions.explanation: Option A: Assembly—The stage where viral genomes and structural proteins come together inside the host cell to form new virions.Option B: Uncoating—The step where the viral capsid is disassembled after entry, releasing the viral genome into the host cell. Triggered by receptor signals, enzymes, or pH changes.Option C: Integration—The process in which certain viruses (e.g., retroviruses) insert their genome into the host’s DNA, allowing long-term persistence.Option D: Maturation—Final modifications of virions to become infectious, often involving proteolytic cleavage or envelope acquisition.

questions.text: Which viruses enter the host cell as a whole?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plant viruses cannot enter as whole particles due to the tough cell wall. Instead, they rely on mechanical injury or insect vectors to deliver only their genome (e.g., tobacco mosaic virus).
Bacteriophages do not enter entirely. They attach to bacteria and inject their genetic material, while the protein capsid stays outside (e.g., T4 phage).

Once attached to a host cell, animal viruses may enter in a variety of ways: by endocytosis, where the membrane folds in; by making channels in the host membrane (through which DNA or RNA can be injected); or, for enveloped viruses, by fusing with the membrane and releasing the capsid inside the cell.
This option is incorrect. An animal virus can enter the host cell as a whole particle.

questions.explanation: Animal viruses can enter host cells as whole particles because animal cells lack rigid walls, using processes like membrane fusion or endocytosis before uncoating their genome. In contrast, plant viruses cannot enter as complete virions due to the tough cell wall; they depend on wounds or insect vectors to introduce only their genome. Bacteriophages also do not enter entirely—they attach to bacteria and inject their genetic material while the capsid remains outside.

questions.text: The branch that deals with the study of viruses is known as what?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Entomology is the study of insects and their relationship to humans, the environment, and other organisms. So this option is not correct.

Virology is the scientific discipline concerned with the study of the biology of viruses and viral diseases, including the distribution, biochemistry, physiology, molecular biology, ecology, evolution, and clinical aspects of viruses.

Epidemiology is the “study of distribution and determinants of health-related states among specified populations and the application of that study to the control of health problems.” So this option is ruled out.
Mycology is the branch of biology that deals with the study of fungi, including their classification, structure, genetics, ecology, and roles in medicine, industry, and the environment.

questions.explanation: Virology is the scientific discipline concerned with the study of the biology of viruses and viral diseases, including the distribution, biochemistry, physiology, molecular biology, ecology, evolution, and clinical aspects of viruses. While all other options are incorrect.

questions.text: According to lwanowsky what are soluble living germs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteria are not typically described as soluble living germs. They are single-celled organisms that vary in their living environments but do not dissolve in liquid.
Viruses are considered soluble living germs because they are not cellular and can diffuse through liquid media. They are not alive in the traditional sense but can replicate within host cells.
Fungi are complex, multicellular organisms that form structures like moulds and mushrooms and are not typically referred to as soluble. They do not fit the description of soluble living germs.
While both bacteria and viruses are germs, only viruses are typically referred to as soluble living germs. Bacteria do not dissolve in liquid media in the way that viruses can.

questions.explanation: According to Dmitri Ivanovsky (1892), the discoverer of the first virus (tobacco mosaic virus), the infectious agent that passed through porcelain filters (which normally trapped bacteria) was described as “soluble living germs.”By this, he meant that the disease-causing agent was so small it could pass through bacterial filters, appearing to be a contagious, soluble substance rather than a typical microorganism. Later, this “soluble living germ” was recognized as a virus.

questions.text: When was the bacteriophage phenomenon rediscovered by D'Herelle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Félix D'Herelle rediscovered the bacteriophage phenomenon in 1917, not 1918.

The bacteriophage phenomenon was rediscovered by Félix d’Herelle in 1917.He observed clear spots (plaques) on bacterial lawns, which he correctly interpreted as being caused by an infectious agent that specifically destroyed bacteria — later named bacteriophage (“bacteria eater”).
This option is incorrect because Félix D'Herelle rediscovered the bacteriophage phenomenon in 1917, not 1920.
This option is incorrect because Félix D'Herelle rediscovered the bacteriophage phenomenon much earlier, in 1917, not in 1990.

questions.explanation: Félix d'Hérelle, a French-Canadian microbiologist, is credited with the discovery of bacteriophages and the recognition of the phenomenon known as phage therapy. He first observed the lytic activity against bacteria in 1917, during his work at the Pasteur Institute in Paris. D'Hérelle's groundbreaking discovery of bacteriophages and their ability to infect and kill bacteria laid the foundation for the field of virology.

questions.text: When were bacteriophages discovered by Twort?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Frederick W. Twort, a British bacteriologist, first discovered the bacteriophage phenomenon in 1915.He observed a “glassy transformation” in colonies of Staphylococcus and suggested it was caused by a filterable virus-like agent that could destroy bacteria.
The option is not correct. Bacteriophages were discovered by Frederick Twort in 1915, not 1920.
Frederick W. Twort, a British bacteriologist, first discovered the bacteriophage phenomenon in 1915.
Bacteriophages were discovered independently by Frederick W. Twort in Great Britain (1915) and Félix d'Hérelle in France (1917).

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages were first discovered by Frederick W. Twort in 1915. While working with Staphylococcus colonies, he noticed that some of them suddenly became transparent and failed to grow. Twort suggested that this was caused by a “filterable virus” capable of destroying bacteria, although he was uncertain and did not pursue the finding further.

questions.text: Virus that was discovered in 1901:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In 1901, the yellow fever virus was discovered by Walter Reed and his team, making it the first human virus identified. Their work showed that mosquitoes (Aedes aegypti) transmit the disease, marking a major milestone in understanding viral infections in humans.

Two scientists contributed to the discovery of the first virus, the tobacco mosaic virus. Ivanoski reported in 1892 that extracts from infected leaves were still infectious after filtration through a Chamberland filter candle.
Bacteriophages were first discovered in 1915 by William Twort, and in 1917, Felix d'Herelle realized that they had the potential to kill bacteria.

COVID-19 first appeared on a small scale in November 2019, with the first large cluster appearing in Wuhan, China, in December 2019. It was first thought that SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19, made the jump to humans at one of Wuhan, China's, open-air “wet markets.”

questions.explanation: The yellow fever virus originated in Africa and was brought to the Western Hemisphere during the slave trade era, with the first epidemic reported in 1901 in the Yucatan.

questions.text: It is very stable and allows viruses to exist in water, air, and the ground.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nucleoproteins generally refer to proteins associated with nucleic acids (DNA or RNA). They play a role in the structure and function of nucleic acids but are not necessarily responsible for the stability of viruses in the environment.

The nucleocapsid is a structure composed of the viral nucleic acid (genome) and the protein coat (capsid) surrounding it. It plays a crucial role in protecting the viral genome. Nucleocapsids contribute to the stability of viruses and their ability to exist in different environments.
The tail is a feature found in some viruses, particularly bacteriophages. It is not typically associated with the stability of viruses in the environment.

The capsid is the protein coat that surrounds the viral genome, forming the nucleocapsid. It is crucial for protecting the genetic material and maintaining the integrity of the virus. The capsid contributes to the stability of viruses in various environments, allowing them to persist in water, air, and on the ground.

questions.explanation: a. Nucleoproteins—Proteins bound to nucleic acids; important for genome function but not for environmental stability.b. Nucleocapsid—Viral genome plus capsid; protects the genome, but stability mainly comes from the capsid.c. Tail of virus—Found in some bacteriophages; used for attachment and injection, not for stability.d. Capsid (Correct Answer)—Protein shell protecting the viral genome; crucial for stability and survival in water, air, and soil.

questions.text: The complete, mature, and infectious particle is known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A primary function of the capsid is to protect the viral genome from environmental conditions and ultimately to deliver the genome to the interior of a homologous host cell.

The virion, that is, the complete infectious virus particle, includes a genome comprising one or a few molecules of either DNA or RNA, surrounded by a morphologically defined protein coat, the capsid; the capsid and the enclosed nucleic acid together constitute the nucleocapsid.

Bacteriophages, also known as phages, are viruses that infect and replicate only in bacterial cells. They are ubiquitous in the environment and are recognized as the most abundant biological agent on earth. They are extremely diverse in size, morphology, and genomic organization.

A nucleus, as related to genomics, is the membrane-enclosed organelle within a cell that contains the chromosomes. An array of holes, or pores, in the nuclear membrane allows for the selective passage of certain molecules (such as proteins and nucleic acids) into and out of the nucleus.

questions.explanation: The virion, that is, the complete infectious virus particle, includes a genome comprising one or a few molecules of either DNA or RNA, surrounded by a morphologically defined protein coat, the capsid; the capsid and the enclosed nucleic acid together constitute the nucleocapsid.

questions.text: A virus is composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Viruses are not made of proteins alone; they also contain genetic material (DNA or RNA).
Naked nucleic acids are not viruses; they must be enclosed in a protective protein coat.

Genetic material can be either DNA or RNA, and it carries the instructions for making more copies of the virus. The protein coat (capsid) surrounds and protects the genetic material. It also helps the virus attach to and enter host cells.

options.text: Nucleic acid, protein coat, lipid envelope
options.explanation: In addition to nucleic acid and capsid, some viruses (like influenza and HIV) also have a lipid envelope derived from the host cell membrane, but it is present in some viruses.

questions.explanation: A virus is not made of proteins alone (a) or nucleic acids alone (b). Its basic structure always includes nucleic acid surrounded by a protein coat, the capsid (c). However, some viruses also possess an outer lipid envelope derived from the host cell membrane, making the most complete answer (d): viruses are composed of nucleic acid, a protein coat, and sometimes a lipid envelope.

questions.text: What is the size of parvovirus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 200nm is too large for a parvovirus. Parvoviruses are actually among the smallest DNA viruses.
This option is incorrect. Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter.
Parvoviruses are non-enveloped, icosahedral particles 18 to 26 nm in diameter. Plus and minus DNA strands are packaged into separate virions in approximately equal proportions.
This option is incorrect and is not the size of parvovirus. Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter.

questions.explanation: Parvoviruses are the smallest known animal viruses, measuring only 18–26 nanometers in diameter. They are non-enveloped viruses with an icosahedral capsid and contain a single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) genome. Because of their tiny size and simple structure, parvoviruses rely heavily on the host cell’s machinery for replication. An example is the human parvovirus B19, which causes fifth disease in children.

questions.text: The viral DNA or RNA is protected by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The capsid is a protein shell made of subunits called capsomeres.It encloses the viral genome, shielding it from degradation by nucleases, UV radiation, and physical damage.Example: In adenoviruses, the DNA is tightly packed inside an icosahedral capsid.

Some viruses have an outer lipid membrane envelope, derived from the host cell’s membrane.It surrounds the capsid and provides extra protection against host immune defences. It also helps in viral entry into new host cells.Example: Influenza virus and HIV have envelopes.

Many viruses coat their genome with nucleoproteins that bind to viral RNA or DNA directly. This stabilizes the genome, protects it from enzymes, and assists in packaging inside the capsid.Example: In influenza, viral RNA is coated with nucleoproteins (NP), forming ribonucleoprotein complexes.

Some viruses hide their genome inside host-derived vesicles or form inclusion bodies within cells. This helps shield viral DNA/RNA from host immune surveillance and enzymatic attack.Example: Poxviruses replicate in cytoplasmic inclusion bodies, providing an extra layer of protection.

questions.explanation: The viral genome can be protected by the capsid, an additional envelope, associated nucleoproteins, or host-derived structures. These strategies help viruses survive in hostile environments and successfully infect host cells.

questions.text: What type of virus is the smallpox virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These viruses have a DNA genome surrounded by a capsid and an envelope. They often establish persistent infections.Examples: Herpes simplex virus (Herpesviridae), Hepatitis B virus (Hepadnaviridae), Poxvirus (Poxviridae), Cytomegalovirus (Herpesviridae).

These viruses have an RNA genome surrounded by both a capsid and a lipid envelope. The envelope helps them enter host cells but makes them more sensitive to drying and detergents.Examples: Influenza virus (Orthomyxoviridae), Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV, Retroviridae), Hepatitis C virus (Flaviviridae), Rabies virus (Rhabdoviridae).

These viruses contain DNA within a naked capsid, without an envelope. They are usually very resistant to environmental stress.Examples: Adenovirus (Adenoviridae), Human papillomavirus (Papillomaviridae), Parvovirus (Parvoviridae), and Polyomavirus (Polyomaviridae).

These viruses have an RNA genome enclosed only within a protein capsid (no envelope). This makes them more stable in harsh environments.Examples: Poliovirus (Picornaviridae), Rotavirus (Reoviridae), Norovirus (Caliciviridae), and Hepatitis A virus (Picornaviridae).

questions.explanation: Viruses are classified by genome type and presence of an envelope. RNA-enveloped viruses (e.g., influenza, HIV) have RNA with a capsid and lipid coat, while RNA-non-enveloped viruses (e.g., polio, rotavirus) carry RNA only in a capsid. DNA-enveloped viruses (e.g., herpes, hepatitis B, and poxvirus) contain a large double-stranded DNA genome with both capsid and envelope, whereas DNA-non-enveloped viruses (e.g., adenovirus and papillomavirus) package DNA only in a protein shell. Notably, the poxvirus is a DNA-enveloped virus.

questions.text: What is the viral nucleocapsid made up of?

options.explanation: "Genome and capsid" is correct because the nucleocapsid consists of the viral genome (DNA or RNA) enclosed within a protein shell called the capsid. This structure protects the genetic material and helps in viral assembly.
"Capsid and spikes" is incorrect because spikes (glycoproteins) are usually found on the viral envelope, not as part of the nucleocapsid.
"Envelope and capsid" is incorrect because the envelope is an outer lipid layer present only in some viruses, whereas the nucleocapsid consists of the genome and capsid.
"Capsomere and genome" is incorrect because capsomeres are protein subunits that form the capsid, but the correct term for the entire structure is "capsid," not just "capsomeres."

questions.explanation: "Genome and capsid" is correct because the nucleocapsid consists of the viral genome (DNA or RNA) enclosed within a protein shell called the capsid. This structure protects the genetic material and helps in viral assembly.

questions.text: What type of virus is the poliovirus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These viruses have a genome composed of two complementary DNA strands, similar to cellular DNA. They usually replicate in the host nucleus and include viruses like adenovirus and herpesvirus. Poliovirus does not have DNA, so this option is incorrect.
These viruses contain only one DNA strand and rely on host DNA polymerase to form the complementary strand. Parvovirus is an example. Poliovirus has RNA, not DNA, so this is not correct.

These viruses have two complementary RNA strands and often carry their own RNA polymerase to transcribe mRNA. Reoviruses, such as rotavirus, are examples. Poliovirus is not double-stranded, so this option is wrong.

These viruses have a single RNA strand, which can be positive-sense or negative-sense. Poliovirus is a positive-sense ssRNA virus, meaning its RNA can directly act as mRNA for protein synthesis, making this the correct option.

questions.explanation: Poliovirus is a single-stranded RNA (ssRNA) virus, specifically positive-sense RNA, meaning its genome can directly act as mRNA for protein synthesis. It does not have DNA or double-stranded RNA, and it is non-enveloped with only a capsid and RNA genome.

questions.text: Herpes simplex is caused by which virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adenoviruses are a group of viruses that typically cause respiratory illnesses, such as a common cold, conjunctivitis (an infection in the eye that is sometimes called pink eye), croup, bronchitis, or pneumonia. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
Poxvirus infections typically result in the formation of lesions, skin nodules, or disseminated rash. Hence, it is an incorrect option.

Flu is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses that infect the nose, throat, and sometimes the lungs. It can cause mild to severe illness and at times can lead to death. Hence, it is an incorrect option.

Infection with the herpes simplex virus (HSV), known as herpes, is common globally. HSV type 1 (HSV-1) is typically transmitted by oral-to-oral contact and causes infection in or around the mouth (oral herpes), but it can also cause genital herpes. HSV-2 is mainly sexually transmitted and causes genital herpes.

questions.explanation: Infection with the herpes simplex virus (HSV), known as herpes, is common globally. HSV type 1 (HSV-1) is typically transmitted by oral-to-oral contact and causes infection in or around the mouth (oral herpes), but it can also cause genital herpes. HSV-2 is mainly sexually transmitted and causes genital herpes.

questions.text: What type of virus is the herpes simplex virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) is a member of the Alphaherpesvirinae subfamily of the Herpesviridae family. HSV is a double-stranded DNA virus that infects a variety of host tissues and is characterized by a lytic and latent cycle.

Retroviruses are RNA viruses with an envelope that replicate through reverse transcription, converting their RNA into DNA to integrate into the host genome. Classic examples include HIV-1 and HIV-2, as well as HTLV-1 and HTLV-2. These viruses rely on their envelope glycoproteins to enter host cells and establish persistent infections.

Non-enveloped DNA viruses have DNA genomes but lack a lipid envelope, consisting only of a protein capsid. Common examples include Adenoviridae (respiratory infections, conjunctivitis), Papillomaviridae (warts, cervical cancer), Polyomaviridae (JC and BK viruses), and Parvoviridae (Parvovirus B19, “fifth disease”). Without an envelope, they are more stable in the environment and survive longer outside a host.

RNA non-enveloped viruses lack a lipid envelope, using their capsid for protection and entry. They are environmentally resilient, spreading via fecal-oral routes or surfaces. Entry is capsid-mediated, with cell lysis for release. Diverse RNA genomes lead to diseases like gastroenteritis (norovirus) and respiratory infections (rhinovirus).

questions.explanation: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) is a member of the Alphaherpesvirinae subfamily of the Herpesviridae family. HSV is a double-stranded DNA virus that infects a variety of host tissues and is characterized by a lytic and latent cycle.

questions.text: The genome of the virus includes:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The genome of a virus may consist of DNA or RNA, which may be single-stranded (ss) or double-stranded (ds), linear or circular. The entire genome may occupy either one nucleic acid molecule (monopartite genome) or several nucleic acid segments (multipartite genome).

The genome of a virus may consist of DNA or RNA, which may be single-stranded (ss) or double-stranded (ds), linear or circular. The entire genome may occupy either one nucleic acid molecule (monopartite genome) or several nucleic acid segments (multipartite genome).

Viruses cannot have genomes made of proteins because proteins cannot store or transmit genetic information. Only nucleic acids—DNA or RNA—can encode the instructions needed to replicate the virus and produce its components. Proteins can perform functions but cannot serve as hereditary material.

The genome of a virus may consist of DNA or RNA, which may be single-stranded (ss) or double-stranded (ds), linear or circular. The entire genome may occupy either one nucleic acid molecule (monopartite genome) or several nucleic acid segments (multipartite genome).

questions.explanation: The genome of a virus refers to its complete set of genetic material, which encodes all the information necessary for the virus to replicate and propagate within a host. Depending on the type of virus, this genome can be composed of either DNA or RNA, and it may exist as single-stranded or double-stranded molecules, which can be linear or circular. The viral genome contains genes that direct the production of viral proteins, including structural proteins that form the viral coat and enzymes required for replication. Because viruses lack the cellular machinery to reproduce on their own, the genome also carries instructions to hijack the host cell’s machinery to synthesize viral components. The size and complexity of viral genomes vary widely, from a few thousand nucleotides in simple RNA viruses to over a million base pairs in large DNA viruses, reflecting the diversity of strategies viruses use to infect hosts and evade immune defenses.

questions.text: What are viruses without a nuclear envelope called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Icosahedral viruses have a symmetrical structure that resembles a 20-sided polyhedron. This term refers to the shape of the capsid, not the presence or absence of a nuclear envelope.

Naked viruses, also known as non-enveloped viruses, lack a lipid envelope. They only have a protein coat (capsid) surrounding their genetic material. This is the correct term for viruses without a nuclear envelope.
Enveloped viruses have an additional lipid envelope surrounding their protein coat. This envelope is derived from the host cell membrane during the viral exit process.
"Bilayer virus" is not a standard term used in virology. The structure of a virus's envelope, if present, is more accurately described as a lipid bilayer.

questions.explanation: Naked virus (non-enveloped): Lacks a lipid envelope; only has a protein capsid around its genetic material. This is the term for viruses without an envelope.Icosahedral virus: Refers to capsid shape (20-sided), not envelope presence.Enveloped virus: Has a lipid envelope derived from the host membrane.Bilayer virus: Not a standard term; viral envelopes are lipid bilayers.

questions.text: Which of the following statements explains why viruses are only able to multiply in living cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Their binary fission is controlled by host cell genes.
options.explanation: Viruses do not undergo binary fission. Binary fission is a method of asexual reproduction used by bacteria and some single-celled organisms. Viruses replicate by assembling new viral particles using host machinery, not by dividing like cells.

options.text: Virus do not possess the necessary components for self-replication.
options.explanation: This is true. Viruses lack the cellular machinery required for metabolism, protein synthesis, and replication. They rely entirely on a host cell’s ribosomes, enzymes, and nucleotides to reproduce.

options.text: DNA is only able to replicate inside living cells
options.explanation: DNA itself is a molecule and can be replicated in vitro in a lab using enzymes like DNA polymerase. The statement is too broad and inaccurate, as it implies DNA cannot exist or replicate outside cells under any condition.

options.text: They have only enough genetic information for DNA replication.
options.explanation: Viruses do carry genetic material, but they cannot carry all the genes necessary for independent replication, including those for making enzymes and ribosomes. Saying they have "only enough for DNA replication" is misleading—it’s not just DNA replication; they lack the whole cellular machinery.

questions.explanation: Viruses can only multiply inside living cells because they lack the cellular machinery and enzymes needed to reproduce on their own. They depend entirely on host cells to provide the tools for replication, protein synthesis, and assembly of new viral particles. Other options are either factually incorrect or misleading.

questions.text: A chemical component that is found in all viruses is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All viruses contain proteins, which form the capsid, the protective coat that surrounds their genetic material. The protein coat is essential for shielding the viral genome from the environment and for helping the virus attach to and enter host cells. Because every virus has a capsid, proteins are a universal component of all viruses.

Some viruses carry DNA as their genetic material, and these are known as DNA viruses. The DNA contains the instructions needed for the virus to replicate within a host cell. However, not all viruses have DNA, because many viruses instead use RNA as their genetic material. Therefore, DNA is not found in every virus.

Certain viruses have a lipid envelope that surrounds their protein capsid. This envelope is usually derived from the host cell membrane and can help the virus evade the host’s immune system. However, many viruses are “naked” and do not have a lipid envelope. As a result, lipids are not present in all viruses.

Some viruses carry RNA as their genetic material, and these are called RNA viruses. The RNA contains the instructions necessary for viral replication within the host cell. However, other viruses carry DNA instead of RNA. Because RNA is not present in every virus, it is not a universal component.

questions.explanation: Every virus has a protein coat called a capsid that protects its genetic material. While viruses may have DNA or RNA and sometimes lipids, protein is the one component found in all viruses.

questions.text: A common polyhedral capsid shape of viruses is a _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Some viruses, such as bacteriophages, have complex structures that don’t fit simple geometric shapes—they may combine icosahedral heads with helical tails. These are called “complex” viruses.

Some viruses appear roughly spherical due to their lipid envelope surrounding a protein capsid. This shape is less about geometric polyhedra and more about the membrane enclosing the virus. Examples include HIV and Influenza.

An icosahedron is a polyhedron with 20 triangular faces, 12 vertices, and 30 edges. Many viruses, such as adenovirus, exhibit this shape because it provides a very stable structure using a minimal amount of genetic material to encode the capsid proteins. It is one of the most efficient ways to enclose the viral genome.

In this shape, the capsid proteins wrap around the viral nucleic acid in a spiral or helix, forming a rod- or filament-like structure. Examples include Tobacco Mosaic Virus and Influenza virus. Helical capsids are not polyhedral because they don’t have flat faces.

questions.explanation: Among viruses, the most common polyhedral capsid shape is icosahedral, which consists of 20 triangular faces forming a highly stable and efficient structure. Helical viruses, although common, have a spiral structure rather than flat faces. Spherical viruses appear round due to their envelope, not because of a true polyhedral capsid. Complex viruses, such as bacteriophages, have mixed structural features that do not fit simple polyhedral forms. Therefore, when considering a classic polyhedral capsid, the correct answer is icosahedral.

questions.text: Identify the true fact about the virus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The viruses were discovered 2 billion years ago.
options.explanation: This option is not a true fact. The discovery of viruses is a relatively recent development in the history of science. The first viruses were not discovered until the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The discovery of the first virus, the tobacco mosaic virus (TMV), is credited to the Dutch botanist Martinus Beijerinck in 1892. The electron microscope, which enabled scientists to visualize viruses, was invented in the 1930s. The study of viruses and their properties has since advanced significantly, but the knowledge of viruses did not exist 2 billion years ago.

options.text: The viruses came from outer space.
options.explanation: This option is not a true fact. The origin of viruses on Earth is still a topic of scientific investigation, and there are various hypotheses about their origin. However, there is currently no scientific evidence to support the idea that viruses originated from outer space. Viruses are considered to have evolved on Earth and are believed to have arisen from genetic elements that escaped from cellular organisms early in the history of life on our planet.

Viruses originated from ‘ancient’ cells that existed before the last universal common ancestor (LUCA) diversified into modern cells (i.e., the three superkingdoms, Archaea, Bacteria, and Eukarya). The origin and evolution of viruses are still subjects of ongoing research and debate in the scientific community. It is believed that viruses may have arisen from genetic elements that escaped from cellular organisms early in the history of life on Earth. Bacteria, on the other hand, are prokaryotic microorganisms that emerged later in the evolution of life. They belong to the domain Bacteria and are considered one of the most ancient forms of life on Earth. Bacteria are distinct from viruses, as they are cellular organisms capable of independent reproduction and growth.

options.text: They can infect all types of cells.
options.explanation: This option is not a true fact. While viruses are known for their ability to infect various types of cells, not all viruses can infect all types of cells. Viruses have specific host ranges, which means they can infect certain types of cells or organisms but not others. For example, some viruses may only infect bacteria (bacteriophages), while others may infect animals, plants, or fungi. The ability of a virus to infect a particular cell or organism is determined by the specific interactions between viral proteins and receptors on the host cell's surface.

questions.explanation: The true fact about viruses is that c) viruses evolved before bacteria. The other options (a, b, and d) are not accurate statements. Viruses were not discovered 2 billion years ago; there is no scientific evidence to support that they came from outer space, and they have specific host ranges and cannot infect all types of cells.

questions.text: What are the subunits of capsids?

Capsomeres are known as capsid subunits, which are the outer protective covering of proteins that protect the virus's genetic material. TMV is also known as the tobacco mosaic virus. Flagella are the organelles for bacterial locomotion. These supramolecular structures extend from the cytoplasm to the cell exterior and are composed of three major structural elements: the basal body, the hook, and the filament.

Hyphae are the feathery filaments that make up multicellular fungi. They release enzymes and absorb nutrients from a food source. Hyphae have a firm cell wall made of chitin. They grow from a tip and extend out in search of more food sources. Hyphae can also branch out and create a large network called a mycelium.
In biology, a septum (Latin for something that encloses; septa) is a wall dividing a cavity or structure into smaller ones.

questions.text: What is the viral envelope composed of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The capsid is a protein shell that surrounds and protects the viral genetic material (DNA or RNA). It is made of repeating protein subunits called capsomeres, forming structured shapes like icosahedrons or helices. The capsid also helps the virus attach to and enter host cells.

The viral envelope contains glycoproteins, which are proteins with sugar molecules attached. These glycoproteins allow the virus to bind to host cell receptors and facilitate entry. They also help the virus evade the immune system by masking viral proteins.
Viral envelop is derived from host cell membrane which is made up of lipoprotein which is lipids and proteins and all.
Viral envelop is derived from host cell membrane which is made up of lipoprotein which is lipids and proteins and all.

questions.explanation: The viral envelope is a lipid-based layer that surrounds some viruses, derived from the host cell’s membrane during viral replication. It is not made entirely by the virus but is borrowed from the host, with embedded viral glycoproteins that protrude from its surface. These glycoproteins are crucial for the virus to recognize and attach to specific receptors on host cells, allowing entry and infection. The envelope also helps the virus evade the host immune system, as the host-derived lipids can mask viral components, making it less visible to immune defenses.

questions.text: It refers to the final changes within an immature virion that result in a virus particle
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Assembly: After de novo synthesis of viral genome and proteins, which can be post-transrciptionally modified, viral proteins are packaged with newly replicated viral genome into new virions that are ready for release from the host cell.

There are all sorts of virus shapes and sizes. However, all virus particles have a protein coat that surrounds and protects a nucleic acid genome. This protein coat is called a capsid, and the instructions for making the protein subunits of the capsid are encoded in the nucleic acid genome of the virus.

Integration is an obligatory replication step for all retroviruses. The process begins after the virus enters the cell and the RNA genome is reverse transcribed into a double-stranded DNA. The direct precursor to integration is the linear form of viral DNA (4, 64).

Virus maturation corresponds to a transition from an initial, non-infectious assembly product to an infectious virion. This process is observed in virtually all well-studied animal and bacterial viruses.

questions.explanation: The question specifically asks about the final changes within an immature virion that make it infectious. While assembly is the step where viral components come together to form the virion, the particle at that stage is usually not yet infectious. Maturation is the process that converts the immature virion into a fully infectious virus, which matches the description in the question perfectly.

questions.text: All of the following descriptions concerning viral multiplication and nucleic acids are true EXCEPT that:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Viruses contain DNA or RNA, not both
options.explanation: Viruses can contain either DNA or RNA, but not both. This is because viruses have a relatively small genome, and it is difficult to fit both DNA and RNA into the viral particle. Some viruses have a single-stranded RNA genome, while others have a double-stranded DNA genome. The type of genome a virus has determines how it replicates and infects host cells.

options.text: Viruses replicate only in living cells
options.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, which means they can only replicate inside a host cell. This is because viruses lack the necessary cellular machinery to replicate on their own. Instead, they rely on the host cell to provide the energy, enzymes, and other factors needed for replication. Once inside the host cell, the virus hijacks the cell's machinery and uses it to replicate its own genome and produce new viral particles. This process eventually leads to the death of the host cell and the release of new viruses, which can then infect other cells.

options.text: Viruses use the cell's biosynthetic machinery to synthesize copies of themselves
options.explanation: Once a virus has entered a host cell, it hijacks the cell's biosynthetic machinery to synthesize copies of its own genome and produce new viral particles. The virus uses its own genetic material, either DNA or RNA, to take over the host cell's protein synthesis machinery. The virus then uses this machinery to produce viral proteins and replicate its own genome. These newly synthesized viral components are then assembled into new virus particles, which can go on to infect other cells. This process continues until the host cell is eventually destroyed and the newly produced viruses are released.

options.text: Viral mRNA,viral tRNA, and viral ribsomes are used in viral replication
options.explanation: The four main types of polymerases used by viruses depend on their genomic constitution and site of replication, that is, nucleus or cytoplasm, and include RNA-dependent RNA polymerases (RdRps), RNA-dependent DNA polymerases, DNA-dependent RNA polymerases, and DNA-dependent RNA polymerases. Proteins include the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), RNA helicase, proteases, metal-binding proteins, and a number of other proteins of unknown function. Genetic studies suggest that most of these proteins are involved in viral RNA replication.So, option D is correct because mRNA, viral tRNA and viral ribosomes are not used in viral replication.

questions.explanation: Option A: Viruses contain either DNA or RNA, but not both, due to their small genome size. The type of genome determines how the virus replicates and infects host cells.Option B: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites and rely on host cells for energy, enzymes, and machinery to replicate their genome and produce new viral particles.Option C: After entering a host cell, viruses hijack the cell’s machinery to synthesize viral proteins and replicate their genome, assembling new virus particles until the host cell is destroyed.Option D: Viral replication depends on polymerases suited to their genome type, such as RNA-dependent RNA polymerase for RNA viruses. Poliovirus uses RdRp to replicate its positive-sense ssRNA genome, making this the correct option.

questions.text: Which of the following is not true for virions?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is not true for virions, as virions can only grow and reproduce or multiply inside a living cell. It then uses the host cell's biosynthetic machinery.

This statement is true for many virions. Virions can contain either DNA or RNA as their genetic material. DNA viruses have DNA as their genetic material, and they use host cellular machinery to replicate and produce new viral particles.

questions.text: What is the function of a viral capsid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Protection against the viral genome from physical and enzymatic destruction.
options.explanation: The capsid is a protein shell that surrounds and protects the viral genetic material (DNA or RNA). This protection helps the genome resist degradation by enzymes (like nucleases) and shields it from harsh physical conditions (e.g., changes in pH and temperature). Without the capsid, the viral genome would be highly vulnerable outside the host cell.

options.text: Providing binding sites that enable the virus to attach to specific receptor sites on the host cell.
options.explanation: Many viral capsids have specialized proteins on their surface (called capsomeres or spikes) that recognize and bind to receptors on host cells. This binding is the first step in viral infection because it determines which cells or organisms the virus can infect—a property known as host specificity.

options.text: Serving as a vehicle of transmission from one host to another.
options.explanation: The capsid allows the virus to survive in the environment and move between hosts. By protecting the genome and sometimes facilitating adherence to surfaces or vectors (like insects), the capsid plays a crucial role in transmitting the virus from an infected host to a new one.
All the options mentioned above are true. Refer below for complete explanation.

questions.explanation: The viral capsid is a protein shell that protects the viral genome from damage and enzymatic degradation, allows the virus to attach to specific host cell receptors, and helps transmit the virus between hosts. It is essential for safeguarding the genome, enabling infection, and supporting viral spread.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a described type of virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a real type of virus. Single-stranded DNA viruses have genomes made up of a single strand of DNA. They replicate by converting their single-strand DNA into a double-stranded form inside the host cell, which then serves as a template for transcription and replication. Examples include the Parvoviridae family.

This is also a well-known type of virus. Double-stranded DNA viruses have genomes composed of two complementary DNA strands. They typically replicate in the host cell nucleus and use the host’s transcription machinery to produce mRNA. Examples include adenoviruses and herpesviruses.

This is a real type of virus. Single-stranded RNA viruses have RNA as their genetic material, which can be either positive-sense (directly translated into protein) or negative-sense (must be converted into a complementary positive-sense RNA before translation). Examples include Picornaviruses (positive-sense) and Influenza viruses (negative-sense).

options.text: Virus containing single strand RNA and single strand DNA
options.explanation: This option is not a recognized type of virus. Viruses generally have a genome made of either RNA or DNA, not both simultaneously in single strands. While some viruses can switch between RNA and DNA in their life cycle (e.g., retroviruses convert RNA to DNA during replication), no virus naturally carries a single-strand RNA and a single-strand DNA genome at the same time. This makes this option biologically invalid.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct, as viruses can be classified into two groups based on their genetic material: DNA viruses and RNA viruses. DNA viruses have a double-stranded DNA genome, while RNA viruses have either a single-stranded RNA genome or a double-stranded RNA genome. There is no known virus that has both a single-stranded RNA genome and a single-stranded DNA genome.

Some viruses are enveloped, which means they have a lipid membrane surrounding their capsid. This membrane is made up of molecules from the host cell that the virus has hijacked. Some viruses are naked and do not have a lipid layer.
Viruses can infect a wide variety of organisms, including animals, plants, and bacteria. Many viruses that infect animals can also infect humans.

questions.explanation: Viruses cannot have both DNA and RNA as their genome because each type requires a completely different replication strategy and enzymes. DNA viruses depend on DNA polymerases, while RNA viruses use RNA-dependent RNA polymerases, and these systems are not compatible within a single genome. Carrying both would be inefficient and unstable, so viruses are classified strictly as either DNA or RNA viruses, never both.

questions.text: Which factors may help determine the antigenicity of a virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Capsomeres are the protein subunits that assemble to form the capsid of a virus. While they are important structural components, antigenicity is not usually determined by individual capsomeres. Instead, the way they assemble and expose antigenic sites on the virus surface is more critical. Thus, capsomeres indirectly contribute but are not the main determinant of antigenicity.

The overall size of a virus does not influence its antigenicity. Antigenicity depends on the presence of specific molecular structures (antigenic determinants or epitopes) that are recognized by the immune system, not on how large or small the virus is. Therefore, size is not a deciding factor in how antigenic a virus is.

The viral capsid, being the outer protein shell, directly interacts with the host immune system. Its surface proteins present antigenic determinants (epitopes) that trigger antibody responses. Since the immune system mainly recognizes structures on the external surface, the intact capsid plays a major role in determining viral antigenicity. This is why the whole capsid is the correct answer.

Proteins located inside the viral capsid are generally shielded from the immune system during infection. Unless the virus is broken apart or during certain stages of replication when internal proteins are exposed, they do not play a major role in determining antigenicity. Hence, internal proteins are usually poor determinants of antigenicity compared to surface components.

questions.explanation: The antigenicity of a virus is mainly determined by its whole capsid, as the capsid surface exposes epitopes recognized by the immune system. Capsomeres only contribute indirectly by forming the capsid structure. The size of the virus has no role in antigenicity, while internal proteins are generally hidden and do not significantly influence immune recognition.

options.explanation: An enveloped DNA virus is a virus that has a DNA genome and an envelope. The envelope is a lipid bilayer that surrounds the virus. DNA-enveloped viruses are relatively rare. Some examples of DNA enveloped viruses include the herpes simplex virus and the varicella-zoster virus.

An RNA enveloped virus is a virus that has an RNA genome and an envelope. The envelope is a lipid bilayer that surrounds the virus. RNA-enveloped viruses are more common than DNA-enveloped viruses. Some examples of RNA enveloped viruses include the influenza virus and the HIV virus.

A naked DNA virus is a virus that has a DNA genome but no envelope. The DNA genome is directly exposed to the environment. Naked DNA viruses are relatively rare. Some examples of naked DNA viruses include the poxviruses and the papillomaviruses.

An RNA-naked virus is a virus that has an RNA genome but no envelope. The RNA genome is directly exposed to the environment. RNA naked viruses are more common than DNA naked viruses. Some examples of RNA viruses include poliovirus and rhinovirus.

questions.text: What does an icosahedral capsid consist of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The icosahedral capsid consists of both hexagonal and pentagonal capsomeres.
An icosahedral capsid consists of both hexagonal and pentagonal capsomeres.
Viral proteins are not triangular.
An icosahedral capsid consists of both hexagonal and pentagonal capsomeres.

questions.explanation: An icosahedral shape, common in viral capsids, is made of protein subunits called capsomeres, which can be pentameric (five subunits) or hexameric (six subunits). Pentamers occupy the 12 vertices of the icosahedron, while hexamers fill the faces and edges, creating a stable, closed shell. This arrangement follows the principle of quasi-equivalence, allowing subunits to adapt slightly to form either pentamers or hexamers. The combination of these capsomeres gives the icosahedral capsid structural stability and enough internal space to enclose the viral genome.

questions.text: Viruses are limited in their host range because
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Can only replicate in certain types of cells.
options.explanation: Viruses require a host’s cellular machinery for replication. Not all cells provide the right environment or enzymes necessary for viral genome replication and protein synthesis. Hence, a virus can only replicate in specific cell types that support its life cycle.

options.text: Certain cells are susceptible to viral infection.
options.explanation: Susceptibility depends on whether a cell has the right conditions for a virus to survive and multiply. Some cells may resist infection due to antiviral defenses (like interferons) or lack of necessary internal factors. Thus, viral infection is not universal across all cell types.

options.text: They can only enter cells that have proper/specific receptors.
options.explanation: This is the main and correct reason for host range limitation. Viruses recognize and attach to specific receptors on the surface of host cells (e.g., HIV binds to CD4 + CCR5/CXCR4; influenza binds to sialic acid). If a cell lacks the right receptor, the virus cannot even enter, regardless of whether the cell has replication machinery.

options.text: They can only enter cells with glycoproteins.
options.explanation: This is partially true but not always correct. Many viral receptors happen to be glycoproteins, but not all. Some receptors are proteins, while others can be glycolipids. Thus, while glycoproteins often serve as viral receptors, the defining factor is the specificity of receptors, not just their being glycoproteins.

questions.explanation: Viruses have a limited host range because they cannot infect all cells. While they need specific cellular conditions for replication (Option A) and not all cells are equally susceptible (Option B), the key factor is the presence of the right cell surface receptors (Option C) that allow viral entry. Option D is only partly correct, since not all viral receptors are glycoproteins. Therefore, the limitation of host range is primarily due to receptor specificity.

questions.text: In which year was the AIDS virus named?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In 1986, the International Committee on the Taxonomy of Viruses (ICTV) officially named the virus HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) to unify the earlier terms LAV and HTLV-III. This name has been used ever since.

In 1981, the first cases of AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) were reported in the United States among young homosexual men who showed unusual infections like Pneumocystis pneumonia and Kaposi’s sarcoma. However, at this time the causative virus was not yet known or named.

In 1983, researchers led by Luc Montagnier at the Pasteur Institute in France isolated a novel retrovirus from a patient with lymphadenopathy. They called it LAV (Lymphadenopathy-Associated Virus). Around the same time, Robert Gallo’s team in the USA also identified a similar virus called HTLV-III (Human T-lymphotropic Virus type III). These names were used before an official consensus.

By 1990, much research had already been done on HIV, including the discovery of HIV-2 in West Africa. However, the virus was already officially named HIV in 1986, so 1990 was not the year of naming but rather the period of further understanding and classification.

questions.explanation: In 1981, AIDS was first recognized as a new disease, but the virus was unknown. By 1983, the virus was discovered independently by French and American scientists and was temporarily called LAV and HTLV-III. Finally, in 1986, the official name HIV was adopted to avoid confusion. Later, in the 1990s, more details about the virus and its types (HIV-1 and HIV-2) were uncovered, but the name HIV remained standard.

questions.explanation: AIDS, or acquired immunodeficiency syndrome, is the most advanced stage of HIV infection. It is characterized by a severely weakened immune system that leaves the body vulnerable to opportunistic infections and cancers. questions.text: For the synthesis of mRNA, HIV uses the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cytoplasmic RNA polymerases are enzymes that function in the cytoplasm of certain viruses, particularly large DNA viruses like poxviruses. These viruses carry or encode their own RNA polymerases, which allow them to transcribe their genes directly in the cytoplasm without depending on the host nucleus. However, HIV does not encode or use such a cytoplasmic RNA polymerase for mRNA synthesis.
This option would imply that HIV does not use any of the above-mentioned enzymes, which is incorrect. HIV clearly uses the host RNA polymerase II for its transcriptional needs.

Host RNA polymerases are enzymes naturally present in eukaryotic cells to carry out transcription. In the case of HIV, after the viral RNA genome is reverse-transcribed into DNA by the viral enzyme reverse transcriptase, this DNA integrates into the host genome. Once integrated, HIV relies on the host cell’s RNA polymerase II to transcribe its genes into mRNA, which can then be translated into viral proteins. Thus, HIV hijacks the host RNA polymerase system.

Some viruses, such as influenza virus or other RNA viruses, encode their own RNA-dependent RNA polymerases, which they use to transcribe their genomes directly into mRNA. However, HIV does not carry a viral RNA polymerase for mRNA synthesis. Instead, its only major polymerase activity is reverse transcriptase, which makes DNA from RNA, not mRNA from DNA. Therefore, this option is incorrect for HIV.

questions.explanation: The synthesis of HIV mRNA does not occur through a cytoplasmic RNA polymerase or a viral RNA polymerase. Instead, HIV depends entirely on the host’s RNA polymerase II after integration of its DNA into the host genome. Therefore, the correct option is Host RNA polymerase.

questions.text: Which of the following is more virulent?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV-2 is a type of Human Immunodeficiency Virus that is less common and mostly found in West Africa, with some cases in Europe and India. It is less virulent compared to HIV-1, meaning it progresses more slowly, causes a lower viral load, and is less likely to lead to AIDS quickly. Transmission rates of HIV-2 are also lower, and many infected individuals may remain asymptomatic for a longer time. Thus, HIV-2 is less virulent.

This option seems to refer to HIV-1, which is the most widespread type of HIV globally. HIV-1 is highly virulent compared to HIV-2, progresses faster, and is responsible for the majority of HIV infections worldwide. It has multiple subtypes (or clades), with subtype A being one of them. Subtype A is common in parts of Africa and Eastern Europe. Although its transmission and progression can vary, HIV-1 overall is more virulent than HIV-2.

HIV-1 is the predominant type of HIV found worldwide and is far more virulent than HIV-2. It spreads faster, has a higher viral load, and more aggressively damages the immune system, leading to AIDS more quickly if untreated. Almost all global pandemics of HIV are caused by HIV-1. Therefore, this is the correct option when comparing virulence.

Here, the option points to a subtype of HIV-2. Just like HIV-1, HIV-2 also has several subtypes (designated A to H), but even its subtypes, including HIV-2(a), are less virulent than HIV-1. They spread less efficiently, progress more slowly, and often remain undetected for years without causing severe disease. Therefore, HIV-2(a) still falls under the less virulent category.

questions.explanation: HIV-2 is less virulent, has a slower disease progression, and is mostly confined to West Africa, with limited global spread. HIV-1(a), a subtype of HIV-1, is more virulent than HIV-2 and contributes significantly to worldwide infections, showing faster progression. HIV-2(a), a subtype of HIV-2, remains less virulent and follows the same pattern of slower transmission and disease development as HIV-2 in general. HIV-1, overall, is the most virulent type, responsible for the majority of HIV infections worldwide, with rapid progression to AIDS if untreated. Therefore, HIV-1 is considered more virulent than HIV-2.

Transcripts are RNA molecules formed from DNA during transcription. In viruses, they are mainly mRNAs that code for viral proteins and are essential for viral gene expression.

Reverse replicase is not a standard term but is sometimes used to describe enzymes that work in the opposite direction of replicase, often linked with RNA-to-DNA or DNA-to-RNA processes, similar to reverse transcriptase.

questions.text: The three stages of HIV infection are
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The three stages of HIV infection are:Acute infection: This is the initial stage of infection, typically lasting a few weeks to a few months. During this stage, the virus is replicating rapidly, and the person may experience flu-like symptoms, such as fever, fatigue, and swollen lymph nodes.Chronic infection: This is the stage of infection that follows acute infection. During this stage, the virus is still replicating, but the person's immune system can keep the virus under control. This means that the person may not experience any symptoms, or they may only experience mild symptoms.AIDS: AIDS is the final stage of HIV infection. This occurs when the virus has severely compromised the immune system, and the person is at a high risk of developing opportunistic infections.Therefore, the correct order of the stages of HIV infection is acute infection → chronic infection → AIDS.
This is not the correct order.
This is not the correct order.
This is not the correct order.

questions.explanation: HIV infection progresses through three main stages. The first stage is acute HIV infection, which usually occurs 2–4 weeks after exposure. During this period, the virus replicates rapidly, causing a very high viral load, and many people develop flu-like symptoms such as fever, rash, sore throat, fatigue, and swollen lymph nodes, although some may not notice any symptoms. Because of the high viral load, individuals are highly infectious at this stage. The second stage is chronic HIV infection, also called the clinical latency or asymptomatic stage. This stage can last for 10 years or longer without treatment, though it may progress more quickly in some people. During this time, the virus continues to replicate at lower levels while the immune system works to control it. Many people do not have symptoms or only experience mild ones, such as fatigue or recurrent minor infections. With effective antiretroviral therapy (ART), individuals can stay in this stage indefinitely and live a near-normal lifespan. The final stage is AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), which occurs when the immune system is severely damaged. This is diagnosed when the CD4 T-cell count falls below 200 cells/mm³ or when certain opportunistic infections or cancers develop. Symptoms in this stage may include chronic fever, weight loss, night sweats, persistent diarrhea, and frequent or severe infections. Without treatment, survival after reaching AIDS is typically around three years, but with ART, progression to this stage can often be prevented.

questions.text: Pigs are related to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pigs are a natural reservoir for certain genotypes of HEV, particularly genotypes 3 and 4. The virus circulates widely in pig populations, often without causing obvious disease in the animals themselves. This makes pigs a silent carrier of HEV.

Hepatitis A virus is an RNA virus that primarily affects the liver and causes acute hepatitis. It is transmitted mainly through the faecal-oral route, often via contaminated food or water. HAV infection usually leads to mild to moderate symptoms such as fever, fatigue, nausea, and jaundice, and it rarely causes chronic liver disease. Vaccines are available and highly effective in preventing HAV infection.

The hepatitis B virus is a DNA virus that can cause both acute and chronic liver infections. It is transmitted through blood and body fluids, including sexual contact, needle sharing, and mother-to-child transmission during birth. Chronic HBV infection can lead to cirrhosis and liver cancer over time. Vaccination against HBV is widely available and is the primary method of prevention.

The hepatitis C virus is an RNA virus that primarily spreads through blood-to-blood contact, such as via contaminated needles or blood transfusions. Unlike HAV, HCV often leads to chronic infection, which can progress silently for years and result in liver cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma. There is currently no vaccine for HCV, but modern antiviral treatments can cure most infections.

questions.explanation: In pigs, swine hepatitis E (HEV) causes microscopic lesions in the liver without any clinical presentation. It is considered important, being a zoonotic disease (spread between animals and humans). Humans can be infected by exposure to infected pigs or by consuming improperly cooked meat. The hepatitis E virus is prevalent in pigs worldwide.

questions.text: The enzyme that plays an important role in HIV pathogenesis:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV does not rely on host DNA polymerase for converting its RNA into DNA; instead, it uses reverse transcriptase. DNA polymerase is important in normal cellular replication, not in the initial steps of HIV infection.

After HIV integrates its DNA into the host genome, the host’s RNA polymerase II transcribes viral DNA into mRNA and genomic RNA. However, RNA polymerase is not unique to HIV pathogenesis—it’s just the host’s machinery being hijacked.
While HIV DNA integration involves joining viral DNA with host DNA, this step is mediated by the viral enzyme integrase, not DNA ligase. DNA ligase plays no direct role in HIV infection.

This is crucial for HIV because its genetic material is RNA, and in order to integrate into the host’s genome, it must first be converted into DNA. This process is unique to retroviruses like HIV, making reverse transcriptase a key enzyme in HIV pathogenesis and a major drug target.

questions.explanation: The enzyme that plays an important role in HIV pathogenesis is reverse transcriptase. Reverse transcriptase is an enzyme that is found in retroviruses, such as HIV. It is responsible for transcribing the virus's RNA genome into DNA. This DNA can then be integrated into the host cell's DNA, where it can be replicated and expressed.

questions.text: All of the following are the current preventive methods of HIV infection, except
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Practicing safe and protected lifestyle choices, such as using condoms during sexual intercourse, engaging in monogamous relationships, and avoiding sharing needles or other drug paraphernalia, can significantly reduce the risk of HIV transmission. Therefore, this statement is correct.

options.text: Use of sterile injections and needles.
options.explanation: Using sterile injections and needles is an important preventive measure to reduce the risk of HIV transmission, particularly among people who inject drugs. Sharing needles can lead to the exchange of blood and potential transmission of HIV. Therefore, this statement is correct.

There is currently no vaccine available to prevent HIV infection. Researchers and scientists have been working on developing an HIV vaccine, but to date, no effective vaccine has been approved for use. Therefore, this statement is incorrect.

Safe blood transfusion methods, including proper screening of donated blood for HIV and other infectious diseases, help prevent the transmission of HIV through blood transfusions. Testing donated blood for HIV ensures that only safe blood is used for transfusions. Therefore, this statement is correct.

questions.explanation: HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) is a virus that attacks the body's immune system. If HIV is not treated, it can lead to AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome). There is currently no effective cure; hence, there are no available vaccines for it.

questions.text: Influenza is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adenovirus is a group of viruses that can cause a variety of respiratory and eye infections.
Poxvirus is a group of viruses that can cause a variety of diseases, including smallpox, chickenpox, and monkeypox.
Influenza is caused by the influenza virus. Influenza viruses are a group of viruses that can cause respiratory illness.
Herpes virus is a group of viruses that can cause a variety of diseases, including cold sores, genital herpes, and shingles.

questions.explanation: Influenza is caused specifically by the influenza virus because it belongs to the Orthomyxoviridae family, which has unique properties — it is an enveloped, negative-sense RNA virus with a segmented genome that determines the typical flu symptoms (fever, cough, sore throat, body aches). Other viruses you mentioned — herpesviruses (DNA viruses), variola virus causing smallpox (Poxviridae, DNA virus), and adenoviruses (DNA viruses) — belong to completely different viral families with distinct structures, replication strategies, and disease profiles. They cause illnesses like cold sores, chickenpox/shingles (herpes family), smallpox (variola), or respiratory and eye infections (adenovirus), but not influenza. In short, disease causation is tied to the specific virus family and its biology, so only influenza viruses can cause flu.

questions.text: This locks the HIV genome into the capsid:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Gag protein locks the HIV genome into the capsid. The Gag protein is a structural protein that is found in the core of the HIV virion. It is responsible for assembling the capsid and packaging the viral genome.
Env protein is responsible for mediating the attachment of the virus to the host cell. It does not have any role in packaging the viral genome into the capsid.
Pol protein is responsible for reverse transcription and integration of the viral genome into the host cell's DNA. It does not have any role in packaging the viral genome into the capsid.

The Gag protein locks the HIV genome into the capsid. The Gag protein is a structural protein that is found in the core of the HIV virion. It is responsible for assembling the capsid and packaging the viral genome.

questions.explanation: The HIV Gag protein is responsible for packaging and locking the viral genome inside the capsid because its nucleocapsid (NC) domain specifically binds the RNA packaging signal (Ψ) and tethers the genome during assembly, while its capsid (CA) domain polymerizes to form the protective shell. In contrast, Pol encodes enzymes (protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase) that are co-packaged but do not mediate genome encapsidation, and Env forms the envelope glycoproteins for viral entry, playing no role in capsid assembly or RNA packaging.

questions.text: In what year did the WHO declare that smallpox was completely eradicated?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This was the year the WHO launched its Intensified Smallpox Eradication Programme, focusing on mass vaccination and surveillance to completely eliminate the disease. It was the start of the final global push, not the eradication year.
