By this year, smallpox had been eradicated from almost all parts of the world. The last known case of variola major (the more severe form) was reported in Bangladesh in 1975. But eradication was not yet declared.

This is the correct answer. In May 1980, the World Health Assembly officially declared smallpox eradicated worldwide after confirming no new cases had occurred and extensive surveillance proved its elimination.
This is after the official declaration. By this time, smallpox was already gone, and global health efforts were focusing on other infectious diseases like polio and measles.

questions.explanation: The World Health Organisation (WHO) declared that smallpox was completely eradicated in 1980. This was a major achievement in global public health, as smallpox was a highly contagious and deadly disease.

questions.text: Which of the following causes mad cow disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prions are abnormal proteins that can cause other proteins in the brain to misfold, leading to neurodegenerative diseases such as mad cow disease. These misfolded proteins accumulate in the brain and cause damage.
Viruses are infectious agents that can cause a wide range of diseases in animals and humans. However, Mad Cow Disease is not caused by a virus.
Bacteria are single-celled microorganisms that can cause various infections and diseases. However, Mad Cow Disease is not caused by bacteria. It is primarily associated with prions.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Mad Cow Disease (Bovine Spongiform Encephalopathy, BSE) is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder in cattle caused by prions—misfolded proteins that damage the brain. It spreads mainly through contaminated feed containing infected nervous tissue. In cattle, symptoms include loss of coordination, behavioral changes, difficulty walking, weight loss, and death. In humans (variant Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease), it causes memory loss, confusion, personality changes, muscle stiffness, and coordination problems, eventually leading to coma and death. Prevention relies on banning contaminated animal feed, strict meat inspection, and culling infected animals.

questions.text: _ is associated with several tumors in humans:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HSV-2 is a type of herpes virus that can cause genital herpes. It is not known to cause tumors in humans.
Varicella-zoster virus is a type of herpes virus that can cause chickenpox and shingles. It is not known to cause tumours in humans.
The answer is oncoviruses. Oncoviruses are viruses that can cause tumours in humans. They are the type of virus that can transform normal cells into cancer cells.

Picomaviridae is a family of viruses that includes the human polyomaviruses. Some of the human polyomaviruses, such as the BK virus and the JC virus, can cause some types of cancer in animals. However, they are not known to cause tumours in humans.

questions.explanation: Oncoviruses (also called oncogenic viruses) are viruses that can cause cancers in humans and animals. They achieve this by inserting their genetic material into host cells, altering normal regulatory pathways, and disrupting tumour suppressor genes or activating oncogenes. This leads to uncontrolled cell growth and tumour formation.

questions.text: The second major form of hepatitis is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis A is a highly contagious liver infection caused by the hepatitis A virus. It spreads through contaminated food or water or through close contact with an infected person. The symptoms of hepatitis A usually start within a few weeks of infection and include fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain, and jaundice. There is a vaccine available to prevent hepatitis A.

Hepatitis B is the second major form of hepatitis. It is caused by the hepatitis B virus (HBV), which is transmitted through exposure to infected bodily fluids, such as blood, semen, and vaginal fluids. The virus can cause both acute and chronic infections, and chronic HBV infection can lead to serious liver damage, including cirrhosis and liver cancer. Hepatitis B can be prevented with a vaccine, which is recommended for all infants, as well as adults at high risk of infection. Treatment for chronic HBV infection may include antiviral medications and regular monitoring of liver function.

Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by the hepatitis C virus. It is spread through contact with infected blood, most commonly through sharing needles or other injection equipment. Hepatitis C can lead to chronic liver disease, liver failure, and liver cancer. Many people with hepatitis C have no symptoms for years, but some may experience fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain, and jaundice. There is no vaccine for hepatitis C, but it can be treated with antiviral medications.

Hepatitis D, also known as delta hepatitis, is a viral infection caused by the hepatitis D virus (HDV). HDV is a defective virus that requires the presence of the hepatitis B virus (HBV) to replicate and cause an infection.

questions.explanation: The second most common major form of hepatitis is hepatitis B. Hepatitis B is a viral infection that affects the liver and can lead to both acute and chronic liver disease. It is caused by the hepatitis B virus (HBV) and is primarily transmitted through exposure to infected blood, unprotected sexual contact, sharing of needles, or from mother to child during childbirth. The infection can range from mild, with few or no symptoms, to severe, causing jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain, and dark urine. Chronic hepatitis B can result in serious complications, including cirrhosis, liver failure, and liver cancer. Fortunately, Hepatitis B is preventable through vaccination, safe practices to avoid bloodborne exposure, and early detection and treatment to manage long-term effects

questions.text: Which viruses can convert normal cells into cancer cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Retroviruses are a type of RNA virus that can convert normal cells into cancer cells. Retroviruses achieve this through a process called oncogenesis, in which they insert their own genetic material into the host cell's DNA. This integration can disrupt the normal regulation of cell growth and division, leading to uncontrolled cell proliferation and the development of cancer.
Adenoviruses are not typically known for directly converting normal cells into cancer cells. They can, however, cause various infections, including respiratory and eye infections.
Polioviruses primarily cause polio, a disease that affects the nervous system. They are not known for transforming normal cells into cancer cells.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Retroviruses are a type of virus that can potentially convert normal cells into cancer cells. Retroviruses, like the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), have an RNA genome that is reverse transcribed into DNA and integrated into the host cell's genome. This integration process can disrupt normal cellular genes or regulatory elements, leading to uncontrolled cell growth and potentially causing cancer.

questions.text: People with chronic hepatitis are at risk of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nephritis is the inflammation of the kidney, which eventually leads towards kidney damage.
Chronic hepatitis is the inflammation of the liver; this leads towards liver damage.
If the heart is damaged or weakened, the heart chambers may stretch and get bigger, and the heart can't pump out the needed amount of blood.
Pneumonitis is a general term that refers to inflammation of the lung tissues.

questions.explanation: People with chronic hepatitis are at risk of developing serious complications due to long-term liver inflammation and damage. The major risks include cirrhosis (scarring of the liver), liver failure, and hepatocellular carcinoma (primary liver cancer). Chronic hepatitis also increases the chance of portal hypertension, which can lead to complications such as variceal bleeding, ascites, and hepatic encephalopathy. Therefore, individuals with chronic hepatitis require regular monitoring and medical management to reduce these risks.

questions.text: Retroviruses are characterized by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Retroviruses (oncoviruses) are spherical in form, about 100 nm in diameter, and enveloped by host plasma membrane.
Retroviruses possess a capsid; hence, this is the wrong option. A capsid is made up of protein subunits known as capsomeres. The number of capsomeres is characteristic of a particular virus.
Retroviruses are a unique group of RNA viruses that can reverse transcribe their RNA genome into DNA; that's how they are characterized.
Retroviruses are a unique group of RNA viruses and hence do not have DNA.

questions.explanation: Retroviruses are a unique group of RNA viruses that can reverse transcribe their RNA genome into DNA; that's how they are characterised. They are enveloped viruses and have a capsid.

questions.text: Bacteriophages, or phages, are also known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term does not accurately describe bacteriophages. Bacteriophages are viruses that infect and replicate within bacteria but are not facilitators; they are agents of infection.
This is a colloquial and somewhat descriptive term for bacteriophages. Bacteriophages are indeed viruses that "eat" or infect bacteria by injecting their genetic material into bacterial cells.
This term refers to viruses that infect animals. Bacteriophages specifically infect bacteria, not animals.
This term refers to viruses that infect plants. Bacteriophages specifically target bacteria and do not infect plants.

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages are viruses that infect and replicate within bacteria, not facilitators. They are sometimes called “bacteria eaters,” a colloquial but descriptive term since they inject their genetic material into bacterial cells. They are distinct from animal and plant viruses, which infect animals and plants respectively.Correct Answer: b. Bacteria eater.Summary: Bacteriophages specifically infect bacteria, and the term “bacteria eater” effectively describes their role.

questions.text: The bacteriophage incorporates into the host genome in which phase?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During the lysogenic cycle, a bacteriophage (a virus that infects bacteria) incorporates its viral genome into the host bacterial genome. This integration is known as lysogeny, and the bacteriophage is referred to as a prophage when it is integrated.In the lysogenic cycle, the viral DNA becomes a part of the bacterial DNA and is replicated along with the host genome during cell division. The prophage remains dormant within the host bacterial cell without causing immediate harm or lysis. The host cell continues to divide and pass on the integrated viral DNA to its progeny.

In the lytic cycle, the bacteriophage DNA does not integrate into the host DNA. Instead, the bacteriophage DNA replicates and produces new phages. The new phages then burst out of the host cell, killing it.
This option is incorrect. Only option A is correct.
This option is incorrect. Option A is correct.

questions.explanation: The bacteriophage incorporates into the host genome in the lysogenic cycle. In the lysogenic cycle, the bacteriophage DNA integrates into the host DNA and becomes a prophage. The prophage is then replicated along with the host DNA and is passed on to daughter cells when the host cell divides. The prophage can remain dormant in the host cell for many generations, or it can be triggered to enter the lytic cycle and produce new phages.

questions.text: How many bacteriophages are formed after 25 minutes of initial infection?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect because bacteriophage replication involves a lytic cycle that produces a much larger burst size. Typically, a single infected bacterial cell releases hundreds of new phage particles, not just a few dozen.
200 is the amount of bacteriophages produced after 25 minutes of initial infection.

This is incorrect because such a low number would not represent the efficiency of bacteriophage replication. Ten phages would not be sufficient for effective infection and spread within a bacterial population.
This is also incorrect, as the burst size of bacteriophages like T4 is not this high. While some phages may vary in burst size, 1000 is far above the typical number produced after 25 minutes.

questions.explanation: After 25 minutes of initial infection, about 200 bacteriophages are formed, which corresponds to the burst size of bacteriophage T4. Options with much smaller or much larger numbers (10, 50, 1000) are incorrect because they do not match the typical replication output of bacteriophages.

questions.text: What is the first step in the replication of a bacteriophage?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is wrong, as the bacteriophage first attaches itself to the host cell, and then the process of replication starts.
This is wrong, as the bacteriophage first attaches itself to the host cell. It then penetrates and injects itself into the nucleus of the host cell.
This is the correct option, as the bacteriophage first needs to attach to the host cell to replicate.
This is wrong, as the bacteriophage first attaches itself to the host cell. It then penetrates and injects itself into the nucleus of the host cell.

questions.explanation: The bacteriophage first attaches itself to the host cell. It then penetrates and injects itself into the nucleus of the host cell. It then replicates the genetic material.

questions.text: Viruses that attack bacteria are called what?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Virophages are viruses that infect other viruses. They are small viruses that depend on co-infection of host cells with a helper virus (usually a giant virus) to replicate and complete their life cycle. Virophages are not specifically known to attack bacteria.
"Lysophage" is not a standard term in virology. It seems to be a combination of "lyso-" from lysogenic and "phage" from bacteriophage, but it does not represent a well-defined category of viruses.

Bacteriophages are viruses that specifically infect and replicate within bacterial cells. They are often referred to simply as phages. Bacteriophages are composed of genetic material (either DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat, and they have specialized mechanisms to attach to and enter bacterial cells. Once inside the bacterium, they take control of the host's cellular machinery to produce new phage particles, which eventually leads to the lysis (rupture) of the bacterial cell and the release of new phages to infect other bacteria.
As explained above, the correct term for viruses that attack bacteria is "bacteriophage."

questions.explanation: A bacteriophage is a type of virus that infects bacteria. In fact, the word "bacteriophage" literally means "bacteria eater," because bacteriophages destroy their host cells.

questions.text: Where is the double-stranded DNA of the bacteriophage found?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tail fibers are specialized structures used by bacteriophages to recognize and attach to bacterial receptors. They play no role in storing or carrying the DNA.
This is incorrect because the DNA is only injected into the bacterial cytoplasm after infection begins. Before infection, the DNA remains inside the phage head (capsid).
The baseplate is located at the bottom of the phage tail and functions in anchoring the phage to the bacterial surface and initiating DNA injection, but it does not contain genetic material.

The double-stranded DNA of a bacteriophage is tightly packed inside the protein coat of its head, also called the capsid. The capsid protects the genetic material until the phage injects it into a bacterial cell during infection.

questions.explanation: The double-stranded DNA of the bacteriophage is found in the head (capsid). Tail fibers and the base plate are structural components for attachment, while the bacterial cytoplasm only receives the DNA after injection. Thus, the correct location before infection is the capsid.

questions.text: What is the phage that causes the lytic cycle called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The phage that causes the lytic cycle is called a lytic phage or virulent phage. These phages have a specific life cycle in which they attach to a host cell, inject their genetic material (usually DNA) into the host cell, and hijack the host's cellular machinery to produce new phage particles. Eventually, the host cell bursts open, or lyses, releasing the newly produced phages to infect other cells. This cycle can occur relatively quickly, often within a matter of minutes or hours, and results in the death of the infected host cell.

The phage that causes the lytic cycle is called a lytic phage or virulent phage. These phages have a specific life cycle in which they attach to a host cell, inject their genetic material (usually DNA) into the host cell, and hijack the host's cellular machinery to produce new phage particles. Eventually, the host cell bursts open, or lyses, releasing the newly produced phages to infect other cells. This cycle can occur relatively quickly, often within a matter of minutes or hours, and results in the death of the infected host cell.

A temperate phage is a type of bacteriophage, or virus that infects bacteria, that is capable of undergoing both the lytic and lysogenic life cycles. Unlike lytic phages, which always enter the lytic cycle and cause the host cell to lyse and die, temperate phages can choose to enter the lysogenic cycle and integrate their genetic material into the host cell's chromosome, becoming a prophage.
Thus, option D is correct.

questions.explanation: A lytic (virulent) phage infects a bacterium, injects its DNA, uses the host machinery to produce new phages, and causes the cell to burst (lysis), releasing many new particles. In contrast, a temperate phage can follow either the lytic cycle or the lysogenic cycle, where its DNA integrates into the host chromosome as a prophage without immediately killing the cell.

questions.text: During the lytic cycle, how many phages are released from the infected host cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.
Cell bursting, or lysis, releases hundreds of new phages, which can find and infect other host cells nearby. From 100 to 200 bacteriophages may be produced per infected bacterium.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: During the lytic cycle of bacteriophages, the host bacterial cell is eventually lysed (burst open), releasing numerous new phage particles into the surrounding environment. The actual number of phages produced per infected bacterium can vary, but as you mentioned, estimates often range from around 100 to 200 phages. This high burst size is a characteristic feature of the lytic cycle. The virus takes advantage of the host cell's machinery to produce a large number of progeny phages, ensuring the efficient spread of the viral infection to neighboring bacterial cells. The released phages can then go on to infect new host cells and continue the cycle.

questions.text: Bacteriophages have been used widely in genetic research since they are the smallest and simplest biological entities capable of what?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bacteriophages have been widely used in genetic research because they can easily replicate inside host cells. Bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria, are useful in the study of how genes function. The attributes of bacteriophages include their small size and simplicity of genetic organization.The most intensively studied bacteriophage is the phage called lambda.Bacteriophages have been widely used in genetic research because they can efficiently transfer genetic material between bacterial cells. This process, known as transduction, can be used to introduce new genes into bacterial cells or to study the function of existing genes. Bacteriophages are also used in the development of phage therapy, which uses phages to treat bacterial infections. While the process of phage replication can be complex, it has not prevented the use of bacteriophages in genetic research.

This option is also incorrect, as it is vague and misleading in this context. Duplication is typically used in genetics to describe the copying of a gene, chromosome, or DNA sequence. Bacteriophages do not simply duplicate genetic material; instead, they undergo a complete replication cycle involving attachment to the host, genome injection, replication, assembly of viral components, and release of new viral particles. Therefore, “duplication” alone cannot describe the complex process carried out by phages.

This option is incorrect because “self-duplication” is not a standard term in biology. Duplication generally refers to the copying of DNA or specific genetic segments, such as chromosomal duplication during cell division. While bacteriophages do duplicate their DNA inside the host cell, this term does not capture the full process of making complete viral particles. Therefore, “self-duplication” is not an appropriate description of what phages are capable of.

This option is partially correct but less precise. It is true that bacteriophages multiply in number after infecting a host cell, as one phage particle can give rise to dozens or even hundreds of new phages after completing its replication cycle. However, the term “multiplication” is more general and does not specifically convey the biological mechanism of replication. Scientists prefer the term “self-replication” because it more accurately describes the molecular process by which viruses reproduce.

questions.explanation: The most accurate answer is self-replication in a host cell, since bacteriophages can reproduce only by infecting bacteria and directing the host machinery to make copies of themselves. “Multiplication in host cell” is acceptable but less precise, while “self-duplication” and “duplication” are incorrect because they do not accurately describe viral replication.

questions.text: Binary fission occurs in which stage of the bacteriophage life cycle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All infections of bacterial cells by phages do not result in lysis. In some cases viral DNA, instead of taking over the control of the host’s machinery, becomes incorporated into the bacterial chromosome. A phage in this state is called a prophage, and this process is known as lysogeny. In this condition the bacterium continues to live and reproduce normally.
The lytic stage involves the active replication of the phage and the lysis (bursting) of the host bacterial cell to release new viral particles.
This is not the correct answer.
This is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: All infections of bacterial cells by phages do not result in lysis. In some cases viral DNA, instead of taking over the control of the host’s machinery, becomes incorporated into the bacterial chromosome. A phage in this state is called a prophage, and this process is known as lysogeny. In this condition the bacterium continues to live and reproduce normally.

questions.text: Which bacteriophage resembles a tadpole?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T2 bacteriophage does have a head and tail structure, but it does not resemble a tadpole as closely as T4 does.
This is the correct option, as the T4 bacteriophage has a head and a contractile tail, resembling a tadpole.
This option is incorrect, as only the T4 bacteriophage resembles a tadpole structure.
This option is incorrect, as the T4 bacteriophage is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The bacteriophage that resembles a tadpole is the T-even phage, such as the the T2, T4, and T6 phages. These phages have a distinct structure consisting of a large polyhedral head attached to a long, slender tail. The head contains the viral DNA, while the tail serves as a tubular structure used to inject this genetic material into the bacterial host. When viewed under an electron microscope, this head-and-tail arrangement closely resembles the body and tail of a tadpole, which is why they are described in this way. This unique morphology not only gives them their characteristic appearance but also plays a crucial role in their infection cycle, as the head stores genetic information and the tail enables attachment and DNA transfer into the host bacterium.

questions.text: These viruses usually occur in two structural forms:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The HIV virus does not have multiple different structural forms.
The hepatitis C virus does have variations, such as hepatitis A and B; however, they do not have different structural forms.
Bacteriophage has two types, T2 and T4. They both are different, as one does not have a contractile tail and the other one does.
The coronavirus had variations but did not have differences in structure.

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages, like other viruses, usually occur in two structural forms: cuboidal (icosahedral) and helical. In many bacteriophages, these two forms are combined into a characteristic head-and-tail structure. The head, which is icosahedral in shape, contains the viral DNA, while the tail is helical, functioning as a tube through which the nucleic acid is injected into the bacterial host. For example, the T-even phages (T2, T4, and T6) have a large icosahedral head and a long contractile helical tail, giving them the appearance of a tadpole. In contrast, some other phages like T3 and T7 are simpler and mostly icosahedral with only a very short, non-contractile tail. Thus, bacteriophages illustrate how the two fundamental viral structural types—cuboidal (icosahedral) and helical—can exist either separately or in combination, depending on the type of phage.

questions.text: In which step is lysozyme released by the bacteriophage?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the attachment step of the bacteriophage life cycle, the phage attaches to the surface of the host bacterium. The attachment is mediated by specific receptors on the bacterial cell surface that the bacteriophage recognizes and binds to.

Lysozyme is released by the bacteriophage during the "lysogenic" phase, not the lytic phase. In the lysogenic phase of the bacteriophage life cycle, the viral DNA becomes integrated into the host bacterial genome, forming a prophage. The prophage remains dormant and replicates along with the host cell's DNA during each cell division.
The virus injects its DNA into the cell just as the syringe is used to inject the vaccine in this step.

In the replication step, the viral genetic material takes control of the host cell's machinery and starts replicating itself. The host cell's resources are redirected to produce more viral components, leading to the creation of new viral particles.

questions.explanation: Lysozyme is a digestive enzyme, so it will have to be released when the bacterial cell wall has to be digested, which occurs during the process of 'penetration.'

questions.text: What type of phage is a T2 phage?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option refers to single-stranded DNA phages, which have their genetic material composed of a single strand of DNA. T2 phage is not a single-stranded DNA phage; it belongs to another category.
This option refers to single-stranded RNA phages, which have their genetic material composed of a single strand of RNA. T2 phage is not a single-stranded RNA phage either.

T2 phage is more properly called Enterobacteria phage T2. It is a virulent bacteriophage that infects Escherichia coli bacteria. It contains linear "double-stranded DNA" and is covered by a protective protein coat.
This option refers to double-stranded RNA phages, which have their genetic material composed of two complementary strands of RNA. T2 phage is not a double-stranded RNA phage.

questions.explanation: T2 phage is more properly called Enterobacteria phage T2. It is a virulent bacteriophage that infects Escherichia coli bacteria. It contains linear "double-stranded DNA" and is covered by a protective protein coat.

questions.text: Which type of viruses infect E. coli bacteria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T phages (T1 to T7) are a type of bacteriophage that infect E. coli bacteria. These phages are known for their role in genetic research, particularly the T7 phage, which is commonly used in molecular biology studies.
P phages are not typically associated with infecting E. coli bacteria. They are not commonly used in the context of E. coli phage infections.
This is incorrect, as option A is correct.
This is incorrect, as option A is correct.

questions.explanation: T phages carry out a lytic life cycle, which means they directly infect the bacteria and immediately kill it; however, P phages carry out a lysogenic life cycle, due to which they do not cause immediate cell lysis.

questions.text: What is the function of lysozyme released by bacteriophages?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysozyme does not play any role in injecting DNA.

Lysozyme does not play any role in replication. Immediately after entering the host cell, the viral nucleic acid takes control of the host’s biosynthetic machinery and induces the host cell to synthesize necessary viral components (DNA and proteins) and starts multiplying.
In the penetration, the tail releases the enzyme lysozyme to dissolve a portion of the bacterial cell wall. Lysosomes contain digestive fluid that is able to dissolve bacterial cell walls.
All of the above are incorrect, as only option C is correct.

questions.explanation: The lysozyme released by bacteriophages plays a crucial role in the infection cycle. Its main function is to break down the bacterial cell wall, specifically the peptidoglycan layer. This enzyme is usually present in the tail region of bacteriophages, and during infection, it helps the phage inject its DNA into the host cell by locally degrading the cell wall at the site of attachment. Later, at the end of the lytic cycle, lysozyme is again involved in breaking open (lysing) the bacterial cell wall to release the newly formed phage particles. Thus, lysozyme is essential both for entry of the viral genome into the host and for the release of progeny phages during cell lysis.

questions.text: Where does the bacteriophage replicate?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As the name says, it infects bacteria and not human beings.
As the name says, it infects bacteria and not horses.
As the name says, it infects bacteria. It attacks bacterial cells and uses their biosynthetic machinery to replicate.
As the name says, it infects bacteria and not animals.

questions.explanation: Bacteriophages replicate inside bacterial cells.They attach to the bacterium, inject their genetic material, use the host’s machinery to make new phage DNA and proteins, assemble new viruses, and then release them (often by lysing the bacterial cell).

questions.text: What is the phage that causes the lytic cycle called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The phage that causes the lytic cycle is called a lytic phage or virulent phage. These phages have a specific life cycle in which they attach to a host cell, inject their genetic material (usually DNA) into the host cell, and hijack the host's cellular machinery to produce new phage particles. Eventually, the host cell bursts open, or lyses, releasing the newly produced phages to infect other cells. This cycle can occur relatively quickly, often within a matter of minutes or hours, and results in the death of the infected host cell.

The phage that causes the lytic cycle is called a lytic phage or virulent phage. These phages have a specific life cycle in which they attach to a host cell, inject their genetic material (usually DNA) into the host cell, and hijack the host's cellular machinery to produce new phage particles. Eventually, the host cell bursts open, or lyses, releasing the newly produced phages to infect other cells. This cycle can occur relatively quickly, often within a matter of minutes or hours, and results in the death of the infected host cell.

A temperate phage is a type of bacteriophage, or virus that infects bacteria, that is capable of undergoing both the lytic and lysogenic life cycles. Unlike lytic phages, which always enter the lytic cycle and cause the host cell to lyse and die, temperate phages can choose to enter the lysogenic cycle and integrate their genetic material into the host cell's chromosome, becoming a prophage.
Thus, option D is correct.

questions.explanation: A lytic (virulent) phage infects a bacterium, injects its DNA, uses the host machinery to produce new phages, and causes the cell to burst (lysis), releasing many new particles. In contrast, a temperate phage can follow either the lytic cycle or the lysogenic cycle, where its DNA integrates into the host chromosome as a prophage without immediately killing the cell.

questions.text: When was the tobacco mosaic virus successfully crystallized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a fact that the tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935.
Tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935. This option is incorrect.
Tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935. This option is incorrect.
Tobacco mosaic virus was successfully crystallized and purified in 1935. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) was first successfully crystallized in 1935 by the American biochemist Wendell Meredith Stanley.This work was groundbreaking because it showed that viruses could be isolated and crystallized like chemicals, which challenged the prevailing view of viruses as "living organisms" and helped establish virology as a scientific field. Stanley was awarded the 1946 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for this achievement.

questions.text: Which virus is complex in structure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The flu virus is considered to be a rather simple virus.
The herpes virus is considered to be a rather simple virus.

The T4 virus is complex because it has unique features such as a contractile tail. The overall structure of T4, studied with electron microscopy, resembles that of a tadpole, consisting of a head and tail. The head is an elongated pyramidal (having two triangular structures with a common base), hexagonal, prism-shaped structure, to which a straight tail is attached.
All the above is not correct, as only option C is correct.

questions.explanation: The T4 bacteriophage is a complex virus with a highly specialized structure. Its icosahedral head houses double-stranded DNA, while the tail functions like a syringe, using a contractile sheath to inject DNA into the host. The baseplate and long tail fibers help the virus recognize and attach to E. coli, and short spikes pierce the cell wall to enable genome delivery. This intricate design makes T4 much more advanced than simple helical or icosahedral viruses, acting as a molecular machine for efficient infection.

questions.text: Viroids differ from viruses in having:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: DNA molecules without a protein coat.
options.explanation: Viroids do not contain DNA molecules. They consist of single-stranded RNA.

options.text: RNA molecules with a protein coat.
options.explanation: This describes viruses, not viroids. Viruses typically have a protein coat (capsid) surrounding their genetic material, which can be either DNA or RNA.

options.text: RNA molecules without a protein coat.
options.explanation: This is the correct description of viroids. Viroids are small, circular RNA molecules that lack a protein coat. They are unique infectious agents that can cause disease in plants.

options.text: DNA molecules with a protein coat.
options.explanation: This describes some viruses, but it is not a characteristic of viroids. Viroids do not contain DNA; they consist of RNA and lack a protein coat.

questions.explanation: RNA molecules without a protein coat: This is the correct description of viroids. Viroids are small, circular RNA molecules that lack a protein coat. They are unique infectious agents that can cause disease in plants.

questions.text: Which of the following shows a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The polio virus is an enterovirus with a single-stranded RNA genome, but it is not typically associated with a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres.

This virus is known for its distinctive appearance, featuring a coiled or helical RNA strand surrounded by protein subunits called capsomeres. This virus primarily infects plants, causing characteristic mosaic patterns on leaves.
Measles is caused by a paramyxovirus with a single-stranded RNA genome. It does not exhibit a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres; instead, it has a lipid envelope with surface glycoproteins.

Retroviruses, such as HIV, have a single-stranded RNA genome, but they are characterized by the reverse transcription process, where RNA is converted into DNA. They do not have a coiled RNA strand and capsomeres.

questions.explanation: Tobacco Mosaic Virus fits the description you provided. Tobacco mosaic virus is an RNA virus that causes tobacco mosaic disease. It has a single-stranded coiled RNA molecule as its genetic material, a part of which hangs outside the protein coat. The protein coat consists of approximately 2130 capsomeres, which are helically arranged to form a hollow cylinder of about 4 nm diameter.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct regarding sexually transmitted diseases (STDs)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A person may contract syphilis by sharing milk with one already suffering from the disease.
options.explanation: Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum, and it is not transmitted through sharing food or drink.

options.text: Hemophilia is one of the STDs.
options.explanation: Hemophilia is a genetic disorder related to blood clotting and is not transmitted through sexual contact.

options.text: Genital herpes and sickle cell anemia are both STDs.
options.explanation: Genital herpes is a sexually transmitted disease, but sickle cell anemia is a hereditary blood disorder, not an STD.

options.text: The chances of a 5-year-old boy contracting an STD are very little.
options.explanation: Since STDs are typically transmitted through sexual contact, it is highly unlikely for a 5-year-old, who is not sexually active, to contract an STD.

questions.explanation: STDs are primarily transmitted through sexual contact, so the likelihood of a 5-year-old boy contracting an STD is very low due to the absence of sexual activity at that age. Option A is incorrect because syphilis cannot be contracted through sharing milk. Option B is incorrect because hemophilia is a genetic disorder, not an STD. Option C is incorrect because, while genital herpes is an STD, sickle cell anemia is a genetic disorder.

questions.text: A common cold differs from pneumonia in that
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The common cold is a mild viral illness, while pneumonia is a serious infection that affects the lungs.
options.explanation: The common cold is a mild viral illness caused by viruses such as rhinoviruses or coronaviruses. It mainly affects the upper respiratory tract (nose, throat, and sinuses), leading to symptoms like runny nose, sore throat, and sneezing. Pneumonia, on the other hand, is a more serious infection that affects the lungs. It can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi and leads to symptoms like chest pain, cough with phlegm, fever, and difficulty breathing. This makes Option A the correct answer, as it highlights the fundamental difference between the two illnesses.

options.text: The common cold mainly affects the digestive system, while pneumonia only affects the heart.
options.explanation: This option is wrong because the common cold does not primarily affect the digestive system. Instead, it affects the upper respiratory tract. Similarly, pneumonia does not affect the heart—it specifically affects the lungs. While severe pneumonia can sometimes put strain on the heart due to low oxygen levels, it is not a heart disease. Therefore, this option is inaccurate.

options.text: The common cold is caused by bacteria, while pneumonia is always caused by viruses.
options.explanation: This statement is misleading because the common cold is not caused by bacteria; it is caused by viruses. Pneumonia, on the other hand, can be caused by bacteria (like Streptococcus pneumoniae), viruses (such as influenza or COVID-19), and even fungi. Saying pneumonia is “always caused by viruses” is incorrect. Thus, this option is wrong.

options.text: The common cold is usually treated with antibiotics, while pneumonia never requires them.
options.explanation: This option is also false. The common cold does not require antibiotics because it is caused by viruses, and antibiotics only work against bacterial infections. Pneumonia, however, may require antibiotics if it is bacterial in origin. If it is viral pneumonia, supportive care rather than antibiotics is usually needed. Therefore, the claim in this option is inaccurate.

questions.explanation: In conclusion, the main difference between the common cold and pneumonia is that the common cold is a mild viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, while pneumonia is a potentially serious infection that affects the lungs and may be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. The incorrect options confuse organ systems, causes, and treatments, but only Option A correctly states the key difference.

questions.text: Identify the molecules (A) and (B) shown below and select the right option giving their source and use.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A) Cocaine Erythroxylum Accelerates coca the transport of dopamine
(B) Heroin Cannabis sativa Depressant and slows down body functions
(B) Cannabi Atropa noid Produces belladonna hallucinations
(A)Morphine Papaver Sedative and somniferum pain killer

questions.explanation: The given chemical structures (A) and (B) are of morphine and cannabinoid respectively. Morphine is the principal opium alkaloid. It is a strong analgesic. It also has sedative and calming effect. Morphine depresses respiratory centre, it contributes to the fall in blood pressure. Morphine is a very effective sedative and painkiller. It is very useful in patients who have undergone surgery. Natural cannabinoids are obtained from the inflorescence of hemp plant Cannabis sativa, family Cannabinaceae. They affect the cardiovascular system of the body.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct with respect to AIDS?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: HIV can be transmitted through eating food together with an infected person.
options.explanation: Incorrect: HIV is not transmitted through casual contact like sharing food, utensils, or touching.

options.text: Drug addicts are least susceptible to infection.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Drug addicts, especially those who share needles, are at high risk of contracting HIV.

options.text: AIDS patients are being fully cured cent percent with proper care and nutrition.
options.explanation: Incorrect There is no complete cure for AIDS. Antiretroviral therapy can manage the condition, but not cure it.

options.text: The causative HIV retrovirus enters helper T-lymphocytes, thus reducing their numbers.
options.explanation: Correct: HIV targets CD4+ helper T-cells, damaging the immune system and leading to AIDS.

questions.explanation: HIV is a retrovirus that infects helper T-lymphocytes (CD4⁺ cells), which are essential for coordinating the immune system. The virus binds to CD4 receptors and enters the cell, where it converts its RNA into DNA and integrates it into the host’s genome. As HIV multiplies, it destroys the T-cells, leading to a gradual decline in their numbers. With fewer helper T-lymphocytes, the immune system becomes weaker and less able to fight infections, eventually resulting in AIDS.

questions.text: In five-kingdom system, the main basis of classification is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Whittaker’s five-kingdom system is mainly based on how organisms obtain their food. For example, Plants are autotrophic (make their own food by photosynthesis), Animals are heterotrophic by ingestion, Fungi are heterotrophic by absorption, and Protists/Monerans may show mixed modes of nutrition. This criterion was chosen because it clearly separates the major groups of organisms.

Although reproduction is important for the survival of species, it was not used as the primary basis for dividing organisms into kingdoms. Reproduction (asexual or sexual) varies within groups and is more useful at lower levels of classification, not at the kingdom level.

Behavioral traits apply mostly to animals, especially higher animals, and are not relevant to plants, fungi, or microorganisms. Thus, it is not a universal criterion for all life forms, making it unsuitable for kingdom-level classification.
The number of chromosomes is not constant across groups and shows variation even within the same species. Therefore, it cannot serve as a reliable or fundamental basis for kingdom classification.

questions.explanation: In Whittaker’s five-kingdom system of classification, the main basis is mode of nutrition, which distinguishes autotrophs (plants), heterotrophs by ingestion (animals), heterotrophs by absorption (fungi), and mixed types (protists, monerans). Other factors like mode of reproduction, complexity of behavior, and number of chromosomes were not used as the primary basis, since they are either variable, not universal, or less significant at the kingdom level.

questions.text: Major cells that are infected by HIV?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Red blood cells (RBCs) do not have a nucleus or the necessary receptors (like CD4, CCR5, or CXCR4) for HIV to attach and infect. Thus, they are not targeted by HIV.
Platelets are small, anucleate cell fragments involved in blood clotting. They lack nuclei and the required receptors for HIV infection, so they are not affected directly by the virus.

HIV primarily targets CD4+ T helper cells, which are crucial for coordinating the immune response. The virus attaches to the CD4 receptor and co-receptors (CCR5 or CXCR4) on these cells, enters, and replicates inside them. As the number of CD4+ T cells declines, the immune system becomes weakened, leading to AIDS.

Neutrophils are white blood cells that play a role in innate immunity by engulfing pathogens. However, HIV does not primarily target neutrophils because they do not express CD4 receptors. While HIV may indirectly affect their function, they are not the main infected cells.

questions.explanation: The major cells infected by HIV are CD4+ T lymphocytes, as the virus binds to CD4 receptors to gain entry and replicate. Red blood cells, platelets, and neutrophils are not directly infected because they either lack nuclei or do not express the necessary receptors.

questions.text: Attachment of HIV DNA with host DNA is done via action of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The enzyme integrase is essential for HIV infection. After reverse transcriptase converts viral RNA into DNA, integrase helps insert this viral DNA (called proviral DNA) into the host cell’s genome. Once integrated, the host’s cellular machinery transcribes and translates the viral genes, producing new viral particles. Without integrase, HIV cannot establish a permanent infection.

Protease is an enzyme that cleaves long viral polyproteins into functional proteins necessary for new virus assembly and maturation. It acts later in the viral life cycle and is not involved in DNA integration.
Reverse transcriptase converts the single-stranded RNA genome of HIV into double-stranded DNA. However, it does not integrate the viral DNA into the host genome. Its role comes before integrase acts.

DNA polymerase is a host cell enzyme that helps in replication of DNA, but it does not participate in inserting HIV DNA into the host genome. HIV uses its own specialized enzyme (integrase) for that function.

questions.explanation: The integration of HIV DNA into the host’s DNA is performed by the viral enzyme integrase. Reverse transcriptase first makes DNA from RNA, protease helps in viral protein processing, and DNA polymerase belongs to the host but does not integrate viral DNA.

questions.text: HIV can be transmitted by all of the following sources except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: HIV can be transmitted through transfusion of contaminated blood or blood products. Before strict screening measures were introduced, this was one of the major causes of HIV spread. Today, careful testing has significantly reduced this risk.

Unprotected sexual intercourse (vaginal, anal, or oral) with an infected person is the most common mode of HIV transmission. The virus spreads through exchange of body fluids such as semen, vaginal secretions, and rectal fluids.
An HIV-positive mother can transmit the virus to her baby through breast milk. This is one of the recognized routes of mother-to-child transmission, along with pregnancy and childbirth.

HIV cannot be transmitted through mosquito bites. The virus does not replicate or survive inside mosquitoes, and blood from an infected person is not injected into another individual by the insect. This is a common misconception but scientifically incorrect.

questions.explanation: HIV spreads through blood transfusion, unprotected sexual contact, and breastfeeding, but not through mosquito bites. The virus requires specific routes involving body fluids for transmission and cannot be spread by insects or casual contact.

questions.text: In HIV, reverse transcriptase converts single-stranded RNA into double-stranded viral DNA. This process is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Transcription is the process of synthesizing RNA from a DNA template. It does not involve the conversion of RNA into DNA, which is the key action in HIV.
Translation is the process of synthesizing proteins from mRNA on ribosomes. This process does not involve converting RNA into DNA, making it irrelevant to the question.
Replication refers to the process of copying DNA to produce identical DNA strands. It does not apply to the conversion of RNA into DNA, which is specific to reverse transcription.
Reverse transcription is the correct process where the enzyme reverse transcriptase converts single-stranded RNA into double-stranded DNA, a crucial step in the HIV life cycle.

questions.explanation: In HIV, the enzyme reverse transcriptase is essential for converting single-stranded viral RNA into double-stranded DNA, a process known as reverse transcription. This newly formed DNA can then integrate into the host genome, allowing the virus to replicate. The other options are incorrect: transcription involves making RNA from DNA, translation refers to protein synthesis from mRNA, and replication is the process of duplicating DNA. Therefore, reverse transcription is the only option that accurately describes the action of reverse transcriptase in HIV.

questions.text: The artificial system of classification was first used by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Linnaeus developed the binomial nomenclature and a more natural system of classification, not the earliest artificial system.
Whittaker proposed the five-kingdom system of classification (1969), which is natural and more advanced, not artificial.

Correct. Known as the “Father of Botany,” Theophrastus (a student of Aristotle) gave the first artificial system of plant classification, grouping plants based on superficial characters like habit (herbs, shrubs, and trees).
Aristotle classified animals into two groups (with red blood and without red blood), but this was not the first artificial system of classification.

questions.explanation: The artificial system of classification was first introduced by Theophrastus, often called the Father of Botany. He grouped plants on the basis of superficial characters such as form, habit, and utility, dividing them into categories like herbs, shrubs, and trees. This was the earliest attempt to classify living organisms, but it was limited because it did not consider natural relationships. Aristotle, though he classified animals into those with red blood and without red blood, did not propose the first artificial system. Carolus Linnaeus later developed a more natural system along with the binomial nomenclature, which improved upon Theophrastus’s work. R.H. Whittaker, much later, proposed the five-kingdom classification in 1969, which was far more advanced and based on natural and evolutionary relationships. Thus, the correct answer is Theophrastus, while the other options represent later or different contributions to classification.

questions.text: The common cold is not cured by antibiotics because it is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the common cold is not cured by antibiotics because it is primarily caused by viruses, not bacteria. Antibiotics are effective against bacterial infections but do not work against viral infections.

options.text: Caused by a Gram positive bacterium
options.explanation: This option is correct because the common cold is not caused by a gram-positive bacterium; it is caused by a virus.

options.text: Caused by a Gram negative bacterium
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the common cold is not a bacterial disease.
This option is incorrect because the common cold is an infectious disease and not cured by antibiotics, as it is primarily caused by viruses, not bacteria.

questions.explanation: The common cold is caused by some 100 types of rhinoviruses. It is one of the most common infectious diseases in humans. Antibiotics are substances that destroy or inhibit the growth of microorganisms, particularly disease-producing bacteria and fungi. Antibiotics are obtained from microorganisms (especially molds) or synthesised. Many antibiotics interfere with the pathogen's protein synthesis. Some (e.g., penicillin) prevent cross-linking of the glycan chains of peptidoglycan molecules in bacterial cell walls. Since the viruses do not possess cell walls or their own protein-synthesizing apparatus, antibiotics do not attack them.

questions.text: Study the four statements (A-D) given below and select the two correct ones out of them. A. The definition of biological species was given by Ernst Mayr. B. Photoperiod does not affect reproduction in plants.C. The binomial nomenclature system was given by R.H. Whittaker. D. In unicellular organisms, reproduction is synonymous with growth.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Photoperiod affects flowering and reproduction in plants.Binomial nomenclature system was given by Carolus Linnaeus.
D is correct, but C is wrong since binomial nomenclature was proposed by Linnaeus, not Whittaker.
Both are true. Ernst Mayr gave the biological species concept, and in unicellular organisms growth and reproduction are synonymous.
While A is correct, B is wrong because photoperiod strongly influences reproduction in plants, especially flowering.

questions.explanation: Among the given statements, A is correct because Ernst Mayr proposed the widely accepted biological species concept, which defines a species as a group of interbreeding organisms that are reproductively isolated from others. B is incorrect since photoperiod, or the duration of light and dark, plays a crucial role in regulating flowering and reproduction in plants, with many species classified as short-day, long-day, or day-neutral. C is also incorrect because the system of binomial nomenclature was not given by R.H. Whittaker but by Carl Linnaeus. At the same time, Whittaker is known for the five-kingdom system of classification. D is correct, as in unicellular organisms like amoeba and bacteria, growth and reproduction are synonymous processes; cell growth directly leads to cell division and multiplication. Therefore, the two correct statements are A and D, while B and C are contrary to the rules and concepts of biology.

questions.text: Nomenclature is governed by certain universal rules. Which one of the following statements is correct according to these rules?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both the genus name and species name begin with capital letters.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Only the genus name is capitalized, while the species name always starts with a lowercase letter. For example, the correct way is Panthera tigris, not Panthera Tigris.

Incorrect. Scientific names are always written in Latin or Latinized form because Latin is a “dead language” and does not change over time. Using local languages would create confusion and inconsistency worldwide.

options.text: The generic name always starts with a capital letter, while the species name starts with a small letter.
options.explanation: This is the fundamental rule of binomial nomenclature. For example, Homo sapiens—“Homo” (genus) starts with a capital letter, while “sapiens” (species) starts with a small letter.

options.text: The author’s name should always be written in full (never abbreviated) after the scientific name.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The author’s name is written in abbreviated form, not in full. For example, Mangifera indica L. (“L.” stands for Linnaeus). Writing the full name is not standard practice.

questions.explanation: The correct option is C because the genus name begins with a capital letter and the species name with a lowercase letter. Options A, B, and D are contrary to the rules of nomenclature.

questions.text: The common characteristic between tomatoes and potatoes will be maximum at the level of their:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Potato (Solanum tuberosum) and tomato (Lycopersicum esculentum) both belong to the family Solanaceae, which is commonly called the “potato family.” Many plants belonging to this family are sources of vegetables, fruits, etc.
They both have the same order, but it will be maximum till family; after family, they both have different genus.
They both have the same division, but it will be maximum till family.
They both have different genera.

questions.explanation: Tomatoes and potatoes share the maximum common characteristics at the family level, as both belong to the family Solanaceae, commonly known as the nightshade family. Members of this family typically show similarities in floral structure, type of fruit, and certain vegetative features. While tomatoes (Solanum lycopersicum) and potatoes (Solanum tuberosum) differ in their edible parts—tomatoes produce fleshy fruits and potatoes produce underground tubers—they exhibit strong resemblances in overall morphology and genetic traits because of their common family classification.

questions.text: Which one of the following organisms is scientifically correctly named, correctly printed according to the International Rules of Nomenclature, and correctly described?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Musca domestica is the scientific name of the common housefly, so it should be written in italics.

options.text: Plasmodium falciparum—a protozoan pathogen causing the most serious type of malaria.
options.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum is a protozoan parasite, one of the species of Plasmodium that causes malaria in humans. Being digenetic, its life cycle is complete in two hosts: man and mosquito. Its sexual cycle is completed in the female Anopheles mosquito and infective individuals called sporozoites are formed. These are transmitted to humans with the bite of an infected female Anopheles. The asexual cycle is passed in man in two phases. Malaria caused by P. falciparum (also known as aestivoautumnal, malignant tertian, or pernicious malaria) is the most dangerous form of malaria, with the highest rate of complications and mortality. In this case, the fever cycle is of 48 hours and is often fatal to the patient as it affects the brain.

options.text: Felis tigris—the Indian tiger, well protected in Gir forests
options.explanation: The scientific name of the Indian tiger is Felis tigris, so the the 1st letter should be capitalized.

options.text: E. coli—full name Entamoeba coli, a commonly occurring bacterium in the human intestine
options.explanation: The full name of E. coli is Escherichia coli.

questions.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum is a protozoan parasite and the most dangerous cause of malaria in humans. Its digenetic life cycle occurs in two hosts: humans, where the asexual cycle takes place, and the female Anopheles mosquito, where the sexual cycle occurs and infective sporozoites are formed. Transmission to humans happens through the bite of an infected mosquito. In humans, the parasite multiplies asexually, producing fever every 48 hours, and the disease, known as malignant tertian malaria, can be fatal as it often affects the brain. The scientific names of some common organisms are house lizard – Hemidactylus, housefly – Musca domestica, Indian tiger – Panthera tigris, and E. coli – Escherichia coli.

questions.text: Which one of the following animals is correctly matched with its particular taxonomic category?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The binomial nomenclature system of naming organisms using a two-part Latinized(or scientific) name was devised by the Swedish botanist Linnaeus (Carl Linne); it is also known as the Linnaean system. The first part is the generic name, the second is the specific name. The zoological name of the tiger is Panthera tigris. So, Tigris is the species name of the Tiger.
Mollusca is the phylum name, while cuttlefish is one of the members of Mollusca's class Cephalopoda.
Human primates are not a family.
Housefly is the name of a species; it is not an order.

questions.explanation: The binomial nomenclature system of naming organisms using a two-part Latinized (or scientific) name was devised by the Swedish botanist Linnaeus (Carl Linne); it is also known as the Linnaean system. The first part is the generic name, the second is the specific name. The zoological name of the tiger is Panthera tigris. So, Tigris is the species name of the Tiger.

questions.text: Which of the following is less general in character as compared to the genus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Species are less general than a genus because they represent the most specific category in classification. A genus can have many species, but each species refers to a distinct group of organisms that share close similarities and can interbreed. Example: Panthera tigris (tiger) is a species within the genus Panthera.

Order is even broader than family. It includes several families that have similarities. For example, the order Carnivora contains the families Felidae (cats) and Canidae (dogs). This makes the order more general than the genus.

Class is much higher in the hierarchy and very general in character. For example, the class Mammalia includes many different orders, such as Carnivora and Primates. Hence, class is more general, not less, compared to genus.

Family is a higher category than genus. It contains one or more genera that share common features. For example, the family Felidae includes the genera Panthera (lion, tiger) and Felis (domestic cat). Since family is broader, it is more general than genus.

questions.explanation: Among the given options, species is the correct answer because it is less general in character than genus. A species represents the most specific unit of classification, describing a group of organisms that share close similarities and can interbreed, whereas a genus is broader, as it includes one or more species. In contrast, family, order, and class are higher taxonomic categories that are progressively more general in nature, since each of them groups together multiple genera, families, or orders, respectively. Thus, compared to genus, species stands out as the most specific and least general category, making it the right choice.

questions.text: Species are considered:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Species are the basic units of classification.
options.explanation: Species form the fundamental building block in biological classification. All higher categories (genus, family, order, etc.) are built upon groups of species. For example, the species Homo sapiens belongs to the genus Homo and the family Hominidae. Without species as the base unit, the entire hierarchical system of taxonomy would collapse.

options.text: Species are the highest units of classification.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because the highest unit of classification is kingdom (or domain, in modern systems), not species. Species are actually the lowest and most specific unit, whereas kingdom/domain groups together millions of species at the broadest level.

options.text: Species are imaginary units of classification.
options.explanation: This is false because species are real, natural entities. They represent actual populations of organisms that can interbreed and share common characteristics. While the definition of species can sometimes be debated among scientists, they are not imaginary but concrete biological groups.

options.text: Species are optional units of classification.
options.explanation: This is also wrong. Species are not optional; they are mandatory in classification. Every organism must be assigned to a species to be placed properly in the taxonomic hierarchy. Without species, the classification system would be incomplete and meaningless.

questions.explanation: Species are a natural population or group of natural populations of individuals that are genetically distinct and reproductively isolated with similar essential morphological traits. A species is also a genetically closed system because its members do not interbreed with members of other species. Species are the lowest or basic taxonomic category, e.g., mango (Mangifera indica), potato (Solanum tuberosum), and lion (Panthera leo). Hereindica, tuberosum, and Leo are species of the genera Mangifera, Solanum, and Pantera, respectively. All other taxonomic categories are defined and described in relation to species. For example, a genus is a group of species, and a subspecies or a variety is a part of a species. New species originate from already existing species. A species is considered a basic unit of taxonomy since, in the greater majority of cases, we do not have intraspecific names.

questions.text: ‘Taxon’ is the unit of a group of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it comes under a taxonomic hierarchy.

"Taxon" refers to all the categories in the taxonomic hierarchy. It may be a kingdom, class, order, family, genus, or species. It is any level of grouping of organisms. Each of these categories has been divided further into intermediate categories like subkingdom,subdivision, superclass, subgenus, subspecies, etc. This term was coined by ICBN in 1956.
This option is incorrect because it comes under the taxonomic hierarchy.
This option is incorrect because it comes under the taxonomic hierarchy.

questions.explanation: "Taxon" refers to all the categories in the taxonomic hierarchy. It may be a kingdom, class, order, family, genus, or species. It is any level of grouping of organisms. Each of these categories has been divided further into intermediate categories like subkingdom,subdivision, superclass, subgenus, subspecies, etc. This term was coined by ICBN in 1956.

questions.text: Linnaeus was credited with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Binomial nomenclature of scientific naming was first given by C. Linnaeus (1735) in his book Systema Naturae and later in “Species Plantarum” (1753). He used two Latin words for any organism, the first being a generic name and the second being a specific name. The generic name begins with a capital letter and the species name with a small letter.

The theory of biogenesis states that life comes only from pre-existing life. In other words, living organisms cannot appear out of non-living matter by themselves. For example, a mouse cannot suddenly appear from a pile of wheat, and maggots don’t just pop out of rotting meat—they come from other mice and flies.
This was done by people like Leeuwenhoek and Hooke, who actually looked at tiny organisms through lenses. Linnaeus never made tools—his work was all about classifying life.
That was Charles Darwin’s big idea, not Linnaeus. Darwin showed how species change over time through natural selection. Linnaeus just organized living things; he didn’t explain how they evolved.

questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature of scientific naming was first given by C. Linnaeus (1735) in his book Systema Naturae and later in “Species Plantarum” (1753). He used two Latin words for any organism, the first being a generic name and the second being a specific name. The generic name begins with a capital letter, and the species name with a small letter.

questions.text: The sequence of taxonomic categories is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because phylum comes before class.
This option is incorrect because the order comes after the class.

This option is correct because, to construct the hierarchy of classification, one or more species are grouped into a genus, one or more genera into a family, families are clubbed into an order, orders into a class, classes into a phylum, and various phyla into a kingdom.
This option is incorrect because genus comes after family.

questions.explanation: According to biosystematics, to construct the hierarchy of classification, one or more species are grouped into a genus, one or more genera into a family, families are clubbed into an order, orders into a class, classes into a phylum, and various phyla into a kingdom.

questions.text: Linnaeus developed a system of nomenclature called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monomial nomenclature refers to naming organisms with just a single word, often used in very early classification systems. However, this method lacked precision and could not distinguish closely related species. Linnaeus did not use this system.

Trinomial nomenclature is used in some cases to describe subspecies (three-part names), but it was not introduced by Linnaeus. Instead, it developed later as an extension of the binomial system to account for more detailed classification.

Linnaeus introduced the system of binomial nomenclature, which is still in use today. In this system, each organism is given a two-part scientific name: the first part is the genus name (always capitalized), and the second is the species name (written in lowercase). For example, Homo sapiens is the scientific name of humans. This system brought uniformity and universality to naming organisms, avoiding confusion caused by local or common names.

Before Linnaeus, organisms were sometimes described with long Latin names consisting of many words (polynomial nomenclature). Linnaeus rejected this system because it was complicated and impractical, replacing it with the simpler binomial system.

questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature of scientific naming was first given by C. Linnaeus (1735) in his book Systema Naturae and later in “Species Plantarum” (1753). He used two Latin words for any organism, the first being a generic name and the second being a specific name. The generic name begins with a capital letter and the species name with a small letter.

questions.text: The basic unit, or smallest taxon, of taxonomy/classification is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The basic unit or smallest taxon of taxonomy/classification is species. Species are groups of organisms that have numerous physical features in common and are capable of interbreeding and producing fertile offspring.
This option is incorrect because the kingdom is the highest taxon.
This option is incorrect because closely related genera are grouped into families, not species.
An order groups together related families, making it an even higher rank in taxonomy. It is used for broader classification, not for identifying individual organisms.

questions.explanation: The basic unit or smallest taxon of taxonomy/classification is species. Species are groups of organisms that have numerous physical features in common and are capable of interbreeding and producing fertile offspring. Taxonomists use species as the starting point for organizing and categorizing living organisms into broader hierarchical groups. The hierarchy of biological classification, from most specific to most general.

questions.text: Which of the following will be curable in the next two decades?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a global health concern, and while there have been advancements in TB treatment and control, complete eradication or a universally effective cure within the next two decades is uncertain.

Cancer may become curable in the next two decades. The completion of the human genome is causing profound changes in the thinking and direction of biomedical research. Cancer is caused by the malfunctioning of genes, either through the activation of cancer-causing oncogenes or through the inactivation of tumor suppressor genes.

Efforts to eradicate polio (poliomyelitis) have been ongoing for many years, and significant progress has been made. However, complete global eradication remains a complex challenge, and it's uncertain whether it will be fully curable within the next two decades.
While science may slow aging and extend healthy life, it’s a natural process, not something that can be fully cured within the next two decades.

questions.explanation: Cancer may be curable in the next two decades. The completion of the human genome is causing profound changes in the thinking and direction of biomedical research. Cancer is caused by the malfunctioning of genes, either through the activation of cancer-causing oncogenes or through the inactivation of tumor suppressor genes. By comparing the active genes in the tumor to those of normal cells, the genes causing the cancer can be determined. Side by side, there is significant progress in the fields of genetic engineering and biotechnology. All these aspects give us hope that cancer may be curable in the next two decades. Tuberculosis is curable by taking antitubercular drugs, and polio may be on the verge of eradication if the pulse polio program succeeds. Aging is a natural biological process, not a disease.

questions.text: Which is the most infectious disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis B is highly infectious because it can be transmitted through various bodily fluids, including blood, semen, vaginal fluids, and saliva.

AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus), is one of the most infectious diseases. It is transmitted through various means, including unprotected sexual contact, sharing needles, and from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding.
The common cold and cough are infectious, but they are generally less severe and easier to contain compared to AIDS. They spread through respiratory droplets and direct contact.

Malaria is caused by the Plasmodium parasite and is transmitted through the bite of infected mosquitoes. While it is a significant infectious disease, AIDS is typically considered more infectious due to its modes of transmission and impact on the immune system.

questions.explanation: Hepatitis B (serum hepatitis) can occur at any age, and its mode of transmission is through contact with infected blood. Infection is severe, often fatal, and is accompanied by symptoms such as loss of appetite, nausea, pale or whitish stool (due to a lack of bile), and jaundice. Even a very small amount, as little as 0.0002% of hepatitis B-infected blood contact, is sufficient to transmit hepatitis B.

questions.text: Viruses that infect bacteria are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A prophage is the integrated form of a bacteriophage genome into the DNA of a host bacterium. It becomes part of the bacterial chromosome and may remain dormant until it is induced to enter the lytic cycle.

A provirus refers to the integrated form of a viral genome into the DNA of a host eukaryotic cell. This term is commonly used in the context of retroviruses, such as HIV, which integrate their genetic material into the host cell's DNA.

Viruses that infect bacteria are called bacteriophages. Bacteriophages are viruses that specifically target and infect bacterial cells, injecting their genetic material into the host bacterium and using its cellular machinery to replicate.

Virions are complete viral particles that can exist outside of host cells. They consist of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat (capsid) and, in some cases, an outer lipid envelope. Virions are the infectious form of viruses that can transmit the viral genome to host cells.

questions.explanation: A provirus and a virion are not the entities that specifically affect bacteria because they refer to general stages or forms of viruses rather than specialized viruses that target bacterial cells. A provirus is the stage of a viral genome when it integrates into the DNA of a host cell, usually seen in retroviruses infecting eukaryotic cells such as humans (e.g., HIV). A virion, on the other hand, is the complete, fully assembled, and infectious form of a virus particle, which can infect a host but is not restricted to bacteria—it may target plants, animals, or humans depending on the virus type. The viruses that specifically infect and destroy bacteria are called bacteriophages (or simply phages). These bacteriophages attach to bacterial cells, inject their genetic material, and use the bacterial machinery to replicate, ultimately causing the lysis (destruction) of the bacteria. For example, the T4 bacteriophage is a well-studied virus that infects and destroys Escherichia coli bacteria.

questions.text: Reverse transcriptase can convert:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the function of reverse transcriptase. The conversion of DNA into RNA is carried out by RNA polymerase, not reverse transcriptase.

Reverse transcriptase is an enzyme found in retroviruses, such as HIV, which enables the conversion of single-stranded RNA into complementary DNA (cDNA). This process is called reverse transcription because it goes against the central dogma of molecular biology, which usually proceeds from DNA to RNA to protein. Once the RNA is converted into DNA, the viral DNA can integrate into the host genome, allowing the virus to hijack the host machinery for replication.
